NO. 22-CI-002878 JEFFERSON COUNTY CIRCUIT COURT DIVISION ONE (1) JUDGE HON. ERIC HANER

DESTINY ADKINS & JUANITA DOWNS PLAINTIFFS

Vv.

STEPHANIE NAIL;

ALLSTATE INSURANCE COMPANY

&

UNKNOWN DRIVER DEFENDANTS

PLAINTIFF’S RESPONSES TO DEFENDANT STEPHANIE M. NAIL 1* SET OF INTERROGATORIES AND REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS

Come the Plaintiff, Juanita Downs, by counsel, and responds to the Interrogatories and Requestfor Production of Documents propounded by the Defendant, Stephanie Nail, herein referred to a ‘Nail’.

GENERAL OBJECTIONS

To the extent any instruction contained in Defendant’s Discovery Request exceeds the language orscope of the Kentucky Rules of Civil Procedure it is objected to and will not be complied with.

To the extent any request calls for information which is protected under the work product doctrine or attorney client privilege is objected to and will not be answered. Nothing in these responses shall be deemed a waiver of work product doctrine or attorney-client privilege.

These answers and responses reflect the current state of Plaintiff's knowledge concerning the documents requested and questions asked. These responses are given without prejudice to the Plaintiffs right to use or rely at any time, including trial, subsequently discovered information or documents omittedfrom these responses

By providing information in response to Defendant’s discovery requests, Plaintiff does not concede that any information is relevant, material, or admissible. Plaintiff reserves all rights to object to the admissibility of any evidence.

Plaintiff reserves all rights to supplement, change, or amend any response, calculation description, or amount contained in any response herein.




INTERROGATORIES

INTERROGATORY NO. 1: Please identify yourself fully. Satisfactory identification could include: Your full name, your current address, the year of your birth, the last four digits of your social security account number.

ANSWER:

INTERROGATORY NO. 3: Please fully identify each Health Care Provider which has examined, treated, evaluated or otherwise been related to your personal health or medical or mental condition(s) (including but not limited to matters related to the accident) since the accident.

For example, a complete identification would not merely refer to medical records, but instead would be specific regarding the following:

a) The name of each such health care provider;

b) The specialty, field of expertise or nature of activity generally conducted by each such health care provider;

c) The date of each examination, treatment, diagnosis, evaluation or other service performed by each such health care provider;

d) The condition or injury that was involved in each evaluation, treatment, etc. of you by each such health care provider;

e) The nature of each treatment, evaluation, service, etc. rendered to or for you by each such health care provider;

f) Whether (in your opinion) an evaluation, treatment, service, etc. was related to the accident in question; and if so, identify each applicable event;

g) The amount of any charge for each treatment, evaluation, service etc. by each such health care provider;

h) The exact amount of money you (personally) paid for each charge by each such health care provider;

1) The exact amount of money any other person or entity paid for each charge by each such health care provider and the identity of the person or entity which paid same.

This information is requested in part so that proponent can comply with MMSEA Section III [42 USC 1395(y)(b)(8)].

ANSWER:

Objection, this interrogatory is overly burdensome, seeks information the Plaintiff is not qualified to answer, as she is not a medical physician and/or medical professional and the information sought is not relevant to the subject matter of this lawsuit and not calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence in violation of the collateral source rule.

Without waiving said objection, the Plaintiff states her does carrier private health insurance through Passport Health Plan under Case ID# [POLICY-1] & personal injury protection benefits under Nationwide Mutual Insurance Claim #617417-GM.

Without waiving said objection, the Plaintiff states she has seen the following providers over the last ten (10) years and refers the requestor to the related medical records for any additional information sought.




University of Louisville Hospital Louisville Metro EMS

UL ER Physicians

ULP Orthopedics

ULP Podiatry

AMG Radiology

KY Injury Chiro & Rehab Gould Discount Medical

KORT Physical Therapy

ULP Physicians (PCP)

INTERROGATORY NO. 4: If you are claiming future medical, hospital and accident-related healthcare expenses, please provide a complete explanation for each such claimed expense.

For example, you may state the injury or condition for which you are claiming same and for each, describe the name and address of each health care provider which has advised you that future expenses will be necessary, the date that each future expense is expected to be incurred, the reason for same and the amount of each said expense.

This information is requested in part so that proponent can comply with MMSEA Section III [42 USC 1395(y)(b)(8)].

ANSWER:

The Plaintiff has completed and/or been released from treatment at the time of this response.

The Plaintiff refers the requestor to the related medical records for additional information in regards to the last date of treatment, examination or consultation.

At this time, future treatment is yet to be determine, however, is likely.

INTERROGATORY NO. 16: Please fully identify and provide detailed information concerning any witness who may offer opinion testimony (indicating whether that witness is a retained expert, non-expert, CR 26.02 expert or lay witness) at the trial of this case.

For example, in your answer you may state the name and address of any and all such witnesses who may provide opinion testimony on your behalf and for each state:

a) His or her education, expert qualifications and curriculum vitae;

b) The nature of any text(s) used, examination(s) or inspection(s) made by said witness;

c) Whether the witness relied on any document(s), report(s), or other tangible things in arriving at his or her opinion, and if so state, with particularity, the item(s) and enclose copies or descriptions of each;

d) Whether said witness has prepared any report(s), or statement(s), and if so, attach copies of same hereto;

e) The subject matter upon which each such witness 1s expected to testify;

f) The substance of the facts and opinions to which each such witness 1s expected to testify; and

g) Asummary of the ground for each such opinion.

ANSWER:

Objection, this interrogatory seeks attorney work product and pre-mature disclosure of Trial Expert Witnesses.

Without waiving said objection, the Plaintiff states that expert witnesses have yet to be determined and the Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement this response in accordance with a court issued trial schedule and/or when expert witnesses have been determined.




INTERROGATORY NO. 17: Please provide any information you have that may tend to prove that Proponent is liable in any way for your damages from the accident.

For example, a proper response could include: any fact(s), information or contention(s) relating to same; when you first became aware of each fact, or item of information and/or contention; how you became aware of each fact, item of information or contention; a description of each physical and/or tangible thing you know of which tends to prove each fact, item of information or contention; and the identity of each person who you believe has information, documents, images or other support for each such fact, item of information or contention.

ANSWER:

Objection, this Request seeks attorney work product and premature disclosure of Trial Exhibits. The Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement this response in accordance to a court issued Trial Schedule.

REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS

REQUEST NO. 1: Photocopies of all documents which may tend to verify the items of special damages you are claiming and further to produce (for inspection and copying) photocopies of all medical records and reports from any health care provider by whom you claim to have been treated, evaluated, offered therapy, diagnosed or examined for the injuries and/or damages claimed in the lawsuit.

RESPONSE:

See the attached. The Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement her production as new/additional medical billing is received. Updated, Certified records & billing have been requested.

REQUEST NO. 2: Photocopies of all documents and tangible things (x-rays, etc.) known to you which in the ten (10) years prior to the date of these interrogatories refer to complaints, examination, treatment, evaluation, therapy or diagnosis of your same body parts which you claim were injured in the accident.

If said documents and things are not readily available to you, please identify each document and thing with such particularity as you would expect to receive if you were propounding this discovery and identify the approximate date of each said document, the person, provider, facility or entity which created it, and the current custodian (name, address and phone number) of each.

RESPONSE:




Objection, this request is unduly overly burdensome. The Plaintiff has disclosed all of her medical providers and their records are equally available to the requestor under his subpoena power.

REQUEST NO. 3: A copy of any and all maps, drawings, photographs, video tapes, motion pictures, statements, or any other item, material or evidence relevant to the lawsuit, including but not limited to, any and all exhibits, repair estimates, repair bills, photographs (or other images) of damaged property, photographs (or other images) of repaired property, computer generated documents/images, photographs (or other images), documents, records, audio records (or other audio memorialization), bills, demonstrative things (writings, objects, drawings, photos, etc.) or other items, including but not limited to those

which you, you lawyer or others may tender, display or refer to on your behalf at the trial of the lawsuit.

RESPONSE:

Objection, this Request seeks attorney work product and premature disclosure of Trial Exhibits. The Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement this response in accordance to a court issued Trial Schedule.

REQUEST NO. 4: If you filed, federal and/or state income tax returns for the five (5) years preceding the date of this discovery, please furnish a copy of all said tax returns. If you do

not have said tax returns in your possession, please complete an Internal Revenue Form 4506 and return it with your answers.

RESPONSE:

Objection, overly burdensome.

Objection, Information regarding tax returns, including income tax returns, W-2 and/or 1099 forms, is privileged under federal and state law.

Without waiving said objection, the Plaintiff reserves the right to supplement this response in accordance to a court issued Trial Schedule.

RESPONSE:

KENTUCKY UNIFORM POLICE TRAFFIC COLLISION REPORT - NARRATIVE 172000.

MASTER FILE # 72614665 AGENCY ORI 0568000 LOCAL CODE [PHONE-12]

Unit 1 was traveling N/B on 24th St and disregarded stop sign. Unit 2 was traveling W/B on Main st. Unit 2 collided with Unit 1 causing both Unit 1 and Unit 2 to collide with the structure at 2401 W Main st. causing

damage to the building and gas meter. Unit 1 occupants fled the scene prior to officers arrival. Unit 1 was

determined to have been reported stolen approx. 30 minutes prior to the collision. WVS active

Assessment Time: [DOB-2855] 23:40:31

Narrative

247a dispatched to mva, on scene, pt was found on side walk with friend, pt involved in mva with multiple air bags deployed side and front, staring on the windshield, pt wasnt wearing seatbelt, dashboard was intact, pt was in passengers seat, pt denied head, neck, chest pain, pt complained of left ankle pain, pt appears to be very anxious, pt screaming and yelling about ever 5 mins that she was in a car accident, pt distracted and talking on the phone, had a difficult time getting pt information, pt was lifted with fires help to stretcher, left leg elevated for ankle deformity/swelling, pt secured to stretcher via seatbelts/side rails and safely loaded into ambulance, blanket placed around ankle to help immobilize with ice pack place on top, pt continued to scream and yell, pt in lots of pain and agitated, pt head to toe

trauma assessed, possible injuries as noted left ankle and left wrist not able to move well, pt then moved both wrists and stated "my wrists hurt too", no other trauma injuries noted, pt transported non emergent to er, upon arrival to er no changes en route, pt wheeled in on stretcher, pt moved from stretcher to bed via bed sheet pull, report and transfer of care given to rn without change in status.

Admit: 7/14/2021 00:12 EDT Discharge: 7/14/2021 03:03 EDT Admitting: ROSS,ADAM S,MD-EMR Attending: ROSS,ADAM S,MD-EMR

History And Physical-Consultation

DOCUMENT TYPE: Consults

RESULT STATUS: Auth (Verified)

SIGNED INFORMATION: SELIGSON,DAVID,MD-ORT (7/17/2021 09:19 EDT); MARKEL,JACOB F,MD-ORT (7/14/2021 01:37 EDT)

SERVICE DATE/TIME 7/14/2021 01:33 EDT

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE MRN:-_ FIN: T[PHONE-2842] Age: 23 Years Sex: Female DOB: [DOB-2841]

Patient seen and examined in the ED. Underwent ORIF R medial mal after ankle fracture/dislocation with Dr. Hartley 4/2020. She states that she had been doing well postop until she slipped at work and twisted her ankle 3 days ago. Has been having pain with movement since. Presents wearing her postop CAM boot. States she had previously been back to wearing regular shoes except when on her feet for long periods of time. Exam largely benign with some tenderness over the Tib Ant tendon. Incisions well healed and ankle stable. Images reviewed show no new fracture. Hardware in good position and fracture appears to be healing very well. Patient advised to continue WBAT with the boot as needed for comfort. Patient to schedule a follow up appointment in clinic asap as she missed her last appointment at the end of June. Ok for discharge from ortho standpoint

Electronically Signed on [DOB-2840] 01:37 AM MARKEL, JACOB F, MD-ORT

Electronically Cosigned on 07/17/21SELIGSON, DAVID, MD-ORT

ED Provider Note *ED

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE MRN: T003085222 FIN: T[PHONE-2837] Age: 23 years Sex: Female DOB: 5/14/1998

Associated Diagnoses: Ankle pain-swelling

Author: IMRAN, AYESHA, MD-FAM

History of Present Illness

The patient presents with rt foot pain after a fall 3 days ago. She reports falling at work gas station, 8/10 pain on onset, today 4/10 on medial aspect of rt ankle. She denies pain anywhere else, headaches, SOB, -LOC. She has a PHMx of rt ankle fracture following a MVA 4/17. She had an ORIF & was ambulating with walking boot. Ortho seen pt in ED. The onset was 3 days ago. Location: right. The character of symptoms is pain. The degree at onset was 8/10. The degree at present is 4/10. Associated symptoms: denies chest pain, denies abdominal pain, denies nausea, denies headache and denies dizziness.

Review of Systems Additional review of systems information: All other systems reviewed and otherwise negative.

Past Medical/ Family/ Social History

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-111] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency

FINDINGS: No acute bone or joint abnormalities are identified. Again seen are 2 fixation screws through the medial malleolus remain intact, without sign of loosening or infection. Well-defined corticated density, adjacent to cuboid bone, likely represent accessory ossicle and/or sequela of prior trauma. The ankle mortise is intact. There is mild soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, finding is more pronounced along the lateral side. No radiopaque foreign bodies are seen within the soft tissues.

IMPRESSION: 1. Soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, more pronounced along the lateral side. No acute osseous abnormality involving the right ankle. *** PRELIMINARY RESIDENT REPORT. PLEASE FOLLOW UP WITH THE FULL ATTENDING-APPROVED REPORT. ***

ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2828] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency

Brief HPI: Patient is a 23 yo female with no significant PMHx who presents to ED c/o R foot pain s/p slipping on something at work this past Friday. Patient was in a MVC in April 2021 and received hardware in her R ankle and R foot for several fractures that occurred during this MVC by Dr. Hartley at UofL. Since this slipping incident, pt has been ambulatory in an old boot from her previous MVC rehab but has been unable to walk without the boot 2/2 pain. NKDA. There are no other complaints at this time.

Medical Decision Making

DDx: Ankle sprain, ankle fracture, hardware failure, ankle contusion

Rationale: All vital signs stable. No evidence of trauma on exam. RLE N/V intact. X-ray negative for acute fracture or hardware failure. Patient received pain medication will be discharged with follow-up with orthopedics. Return precaution discussed with patient.

Radiology Results (Last 48 hours)

72119550008 -- [DOB-116] 00:12

CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT ([DOB-2826] 00:54)

Result: EXAMINATION: 3-view right ankle radiograph DATE: [DOB-2825] 12:45 AMACCESSION: 40CR210064061PROVIDED INDICATION: R ankle painCOMPARISON: Right ankle dated 05/06/2020TECHNIQUE: AP. lateral, and obliqueradiographs of the right ankle were obtained.FINDINGS: No acute bone or joint abnormalities are identified. Again seen are 2 fixation screws through the medial malleolus remain intact, without sign of loosening or infection. Well-defined corticated density, adjacent to cuboid bone, likely represent accessory ossicle and/or sequela of prior trauma. The ankle mortise is intact. There is mild soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, finding is more pronounced along the lateral side. No radiopaque foreign bodies are seen within the soft tissues.| MPRESSION:1. Soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, more pronounced along the lateral side. No acute osseous abnormality involving the right ankle.*** PRELIMINARY RESIDENT REPORT. PLEASE FOLLOW UP WITH THE FULL ATTENDING-APPROVED REPORT. ***

Physical Examination

General: Alert, no acute distress.

Skin: Warm, dry, normal for ethnicity.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic.

Eye: Pupils are equal, round and reactive to light, extraocular movements are intact, normal conjunctiva.

Ears, nose, mouth and throat: Oral mucosa moist, external nose and ears with no gross deformity or discharge.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, Normal peripheral perfusion, DP/PT pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation, respirations are non-labored.

Gastrointestinal: Soft, Nontender, Non distended.

Musculoskeletal: Moving all extremities spontaneously, R foot mild TTP , R medial ankle, no swelling, no deformity , Bilateral dorsal flexion and plantar flexion intact , Pedal sensations intact distally.

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation, No focal neurological deficit observed, normal speech observed, CNs II-XII grossly intact.

Impression and Plan Diagnosis Right ankle sprain - Discharge, Medical Plan Condition: Improved. Prescriptions: Prescription Writer Pharmacy: acetaminophen 500 mg oral tablet (Prescribe): 1,000 mg = 2 Tab, Oral, Q6H, for 3 Day(s), PRN: Pain (Mild 1-3), 24 Tab, 0 Refill(s). Patient was given the following educational materials: Ankle Sprain. Follow up with: Your Primary Care Physician Within 2 to 3 days You have received an emergency department evaluation. This evaluation is intended to rule out life-threatening conditions. You may require further diagnostic testing as determined by your primary care provider or any referred specialist.

Please follow up with your primary care provider within 48 hours. Call for an appointment as soon as possible. Please continue taking your home medications and take any new medications as prescribed. Please follow all discharge instructions.

Please return to the emergency department if you experience fevers, chest pain, shortness of breath, inability to tolerate oral intake, stroke-like symptoms, or your symptoms worsen or do not improve.

Ankle Sprain

An ankle sprain is a stretch or tear in a ligament in the ankle. Ligaments are tissues that connect bones to each

other.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab

Report Request ID: 143290758

Page 14 of 76 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient. DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2803] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency

The two most common types of ankle sprains are:

¢ Inversion sprain. This happens when the foot turns inward and the ankle rolls outward. It affects the ligament on the outside of the foot (lateral ligament).

¢ version sprain. This happens when the foot turns outward and the ankle rolls inward. It affects the ligament on the inner side of the foot (medial ligament).

What are the causes?

This condition is often caused by accidentally rolling or twisting the ankle. What increases the risk?

You are more likely to develop this condition if you play sports.

What are the signs or symptoms?

P"

Symptoms of this condition include:

¢ Pain in your ankle. ¢ Swelling. ¢ Bruising. This may develop right after you sprain your ankle or 1—2 days later.

¢ Trouble standing or walking, especially when you turn or change directions. How is this diagnosed? This condition is diagnosed with:

¢ A physical exam. During the exam, your health care provider will press on certain parts of your foot and ankle and try to move them in certain ways. ¢ X-ray imaging. These may be taken to see how severe the sprain is and to check for broken bones.

How is this treated?

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290758 Page 15 of 76 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2802] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency

This condition may be treated with:

¢ A brace or splint. This is used to keep the ankle from moving until it heals. ¢ An elastic bandage. This is used to support the ankle.

* Crutches.

¢ Pain medicine.

¢ Surgery. This may be needed if the sprain is severe.

¢ Physical therapy. This may help to improve the range of motion in the ankle.

Follow these instructions at home: If you have a brace or a splint:

¢ Wear the brace or splint as told by your health care provider. Remove it only as told by your health care provider.

¢ Loosen the brace or splint if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue.

* Keep the brace or splint clean.

¢ Ifthe brace or splint is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet.

—46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower. If you have an elastic bandage (dressing):

* Remove it to shower or bathe.

¢ Try not to move your ankle much, but wiggle your toes from time to time. This helps to prevent swelling.

¢ Adjust the dressing to make it more comfortable if it feels too tight.

¢ Loosen the dressing if you have numbness or tingling in your foot, or if your foot becomes cold and blue.

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290758 Page 16 of 76 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2801] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency i ~—-- )

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. ¢ For 2-3 days, keep your ankle raised (elevated) above the level of your heart as much as possible. ¢ If directed, put ice on the injured area: —46 Ifyou have a removable brace or splint, remove it as told by your health care provider. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

General instructions

¢ Rest your ankle.

¢ Do not use the injured limb to support your body weight until your health care provider says that you can. Use crutches as told by your health care provider.

¢ Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and chewing tobacco. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if:

¢ You have rapidly increasing bruising or swelling.

¢ Your pain is not relieved with medicine.

Get help right away if:

¢ Your foot or toes become numb or blue.

¢ You have severe pain that gets worse.

Summary

¢ An ankle sprain is a stretch or tear in a ligament in the ankle. Ligaments are tissues that connect bones to each other.

¢ This condition is often caused by accidentally rolling or twisting the ankle.

¢ Symptoms include pain, swelling, bruising, and trouble walking.

¢ To relieve pain and swelling, put ice on the affected ankle, raise your ankle above the level of your heart, and use an elastic bandage.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

As part of your treatment plan, you provider may have prescribed a limited course of a controlled substance. This medication may be given to help people with moderate or severe pain or for other medical conditions, but there are risks involved with treatment. Common side effects may include nausea, constipation, drowsiness, sweating, itching, dry mouth, and rash. More serious side effects may include cognitive and motor impairment, like problems with thinking, concentrating, alertness, and movement (e.g. slowed reflexes), and driving and operating heavy machinery can be dangerous. It is important for you to talk to your provider if you have these side effects or questions. These controlled substances can produce physical dependence and be habit-forming if taken for an extended period of time, which means that the body has gotten used o them and may experience withdrawal symptoms, if they are abruptly stopped. Ankle Sprain

sprained NN ay

An ankle sprain is a stretch or tear in a ligament in the ankle. Ligaments are tissues that connect bones to each other.

The two most common types of ankle sprains are: ° Inversion sprain. This happens when the foot turns inward and the ankle rolls outward. It affects the ligament on the outside of the foot (lateral ligament).

. Eversion sprain. This happens when the foot turns outward and the ankle rolls inward. It affects the ligament on the inner side of the foot (medial ligament).

What are the causes?

This condition is often caused by accidentally rolling or twisting the ankle.

What increases the risk?

You are more likely to develop this condition if you play sports.

What are the signs or symptoms?

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290758 Page 22 of 76 12/3/2021 12:57 EST

Symptoms of this condition include:

. Pain in your ankle.

° Swelling.

° Bruising. This may develop right after you sprain your ankle or 1—2 days later. ° Trouble standing or walking, especially when you turn or change directions.

How is this diagnosed? This condition is diagnosed with:

° A physical exam. During the exam, your health care provider will press on certain parts of your foot and ankle and try to move them in certain ways. . X-ray imaging. These may be taken to see how severe the sprain is and to check for broken bones.

How is this treated? This condition may be treated with:

° A brace or splint. This is used to keep the ankle from moving until it heals.

° An elastic bandage. This is used to support the ankle.

. Crutches.

. Pain medicine.

° Surgery. This may be needed if the sprain is severe.

. Physical therapy. This may help to improve the range of motion in the ankle.

Follow these instructions at home: If you have a brace or a splint:

° Wear the brace or splint as told by your health care provider. Remove it only as told by your health care provider. ° Loosen the brace or splint if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue. . Keep the brace or splint clean. ° If the brace or splint is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet. —46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower.

If you have an elastic bandage (dressing):

Emergency Awareness and Preventative Care

STROKE is an EMERGENCY Every Minute Counts

Act FAST and Check for these signs:

FACE Does the face look uneven? ARM Does one arm drift down?

Does their speech sound SPEECH strange? TIME Call 9-1-1 at any sign of stroke Stroke Risk Factors Atrial Fibrillation . Family history of (irregular heartbeat) Diabetes stroke Heart Disease Heavy alcohol use High Blood Pressure

Physical inactivity

High Cholesterol 9 and obesity

Smoking

Cigarette Smoking

The facts are clear, cigarette smoking will shorten your life. Smoking can cause many illnesses along the way. As a healthcare provider, we recommend that you stop smoking. Assistance with quitting is available by contacting 1-800-QUIT-NOW. This is a free resource providing counseling, support, and referral. Or you may contact your personal physician.

National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 1-[PHONE-2776]

The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline is a national network of local crisis centers that provides free and confidential emotional support to people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

Don't Wait! Stop a Heart Attack Before It Starts

What is a heart attack?

A heart attack is damage or death to a part of the heart from severely decreased or lack of blood flow to the heart. Over time, arteries can become narrow from the buildup of fat and cholesterol, which is called plaque. The plaque can rupture causing a blood clot to form. When the blood clot forms, the artery can become severely narrowed or completely blocked, causing a heart attack.

¢ Heart attack is the leading cause of death in the United States.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290758 Page 25 of 76 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2775] Account #: 72119550008 Emergency

* 85% of muscle damage occurs within the first 2 hours.

* Delay in the recognition of heart attack symptoms increases the chances of death.

Know the early symptoms of a heart attack:

Feeling of Pain that travels Nausea "fullness" in Jaw Pain down one or chest both arms . . Chest pressure, Fatigue/being Anxiety Back Pain squeezing, or discomfort Shortness of | Sweating, ora F ecling oF breath cold sweat Impending doom

There are unusual signs of heart attack, too! Women, the elderly, and diabetics may present with atypical symptoms:

* Fainting/dizziness ¢ Weakness

* Confusion

Risk Factors for a Heart Attack

Some heart disease risk factors, such as age and family, cannot be changed. Others, like smoking and lack of exercise, can be changed.

Smoking High Cholesterol Blood Pressure Family History Obesity Age

Gender (Males are at Lack of Exercise Diabetes

higher risk)

Diet Stress Excessive Alcohol

Intake

If you or someone you know is experiencing the signs and symptoms of a heart attack, DON'T DELAY. Call 9-1-1 immediately and seek help.

If someone collapses, perform CPR!

Do not attempt to drive if you are having symptoms of heart attack.

Why Hands-Only CPR?

* Hands-Only CPR has been shown to be as effective as conventional CPR for cardiac arrests that occur outside of a hospital.

* Survival depends on immediately receiving CPR from someone nearby.

How do you perform Hands-Only CPR? There are two easy steps:

* 1. Call 9-1-1 if you see a teen or adult collapse

¢ 2. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest at a beat of 100 beats per minute

Save a life!

4 WAYS TO GET AHEAD OF SEPSIS

SEPSIS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Time Matters!

Infections put you and your family at risk for a life-threatening condition called sepsis.

* Sepsis is the body's extreme response to an infection. It is life-threatening, and without timely treatment, sepsis can rapidly lead to tissue damage, organ failure, and death. Sepsis happens when an infection you already have-in your skin, lungs, urinary tract or somewhere else-riggers a chain reaction throughout your body.

PREVENT INFECTIONS

PRACTICE GOOD HYGIENE

KNOW THE SYMPTOMS

ACT FAST

ERG

Take good care of chronic conditions. Talk to your doctor about getting the recommended vaccines.

Wash your hands frequently.

Keep cuts or open sores clean and covered until they

are healed.

Confusion or disorientation Shortness of breath High heart rate Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold Extreme pain or discomfort

Clammy or sweaty skin

Get medical care IMMEDIATELY if you suspect sepsis

or if you have an infection that is not

getting better or is getting worse.

To learn more about sepsis and how to prevent infections, visit www.cdc.gov/sepsis.

The examination and treatment you have received in the Emergency Department has been done to provide an appropriate evaluation and stabilizing treatment on an emregency basis only. Given the limited resources, it is not meant to be a substitute for complete medical care. The follow-up provider you named will receive a copy of your records and all test reports. IT IS IMPORTANT THAT YOU SCHEDULE A FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENT AND RE-EVALUATED. You should report any new complaints, symptoms, or remaining problems at that time. IT IS IMPOSSIBLE FOR THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT TO RECOGNIZE AND TREAT ALL ELEMENTS OF INJURY OR ILLNESS IN A SINGLE VISIT.

If you have been referred to a specialist provider, it means that we believe you may have a condition that requires the expertise of a specialist. These providers work in partnership with the hospital and have agreed to see referred patients in their office for further evaluation. KEEP IN MIND THAT THE SPECIALIST HAS HIS/HER OWN OFFICE POLICIES WHICH MAY REQUIRE PROPER INSURANCE OR PAYMENT UP FRONT BEFORE THE SPECIALIST WILL SEE YOU. It is your responsibility to call the specialist provider to make an appointment. We do not have the ability to refer patients to specialists/physicians that work with specific insurance companies.

Please be advised that all financial charges or billing practices are determined by that practice, not the hospital. If your insurance company requires that you see a specialist from their approved list, it is your responsibility to contact your insurance company to make those arrangements. It is also your responsibility to follow any other requirements of your insurance company necessary to obtain coverage for claims submitted.

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2735] Account #: T[PHONE-2734] Diagnostic Radiology Accession Exam STATUS Exam Date/Time Ordering Provider 40-CR-21-0064061 CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT Auth (Verified) 7/14/2021 00:54 EDT ROSS,ADAM S,MD- EMR

Reason For Exam (CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT) R ankle pain

Report EXAMINATION: 3-view right ankle radiograph

DATE: [DOB-210] 12:45 AM

ACCESSION: 40CR210064061

PROVIDED INDICATION: R ankle pain

COMPARISON: Right ankle dated [DOB-211]

TECHNIQUE: AP, lateral, and obliqueradiographs of the right ankle were obtained.

FINDINGS: No acute bone or joint abnormalities are identified. Again seen are 2 fixation screws through the medial malleolus remain intact, without sign of loosening or infection. Well-defined corticated density, adjacent to cuboid bone, likely represent accessory ossicle and/or sequela of prior trauma. The ankle mortise is intact. There is mild soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, finding is more pronounced along the lateral side. No radiopaque foreign bodies are seen within the soft tissues.

IMPRESSION:

1. Soft tissue swelling about the right ankle, more pronounced along the lateral side. No acute osseous abnormality involving the right ankle.

USING AND SHARING MY INFORMATION

The Law: There is a federal law called the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (“HIPAA”). This law requires UofL Health to protect the privacy and security of its patients’ treatment, contact, and financial information. Taken together, this information is called your “Health Information”. There are also other federal and/or state laws that require UofL Health to take additional steps to protect certain categories of Health Information, including, but not limited to, Health Information about behavioral or mental health; developmental disabilities; treatment for substance abuse (alcohol and/or drugs) disorders; genetic testing and counseling; HIV/AIDS; sexual assault/ abuse; sexually transmitted illnesses; pregnancy; birth control; domestic abuse of an adult with a disability; child abuse and neglect.

Authorization and Notice of Privacy Practices (“NPP”):If my permission is required by law, by signing this form | agree that UofL Health may receive, use and disclose my Health Information as set forth in this Consent and as set forth in UofL Health's NPP. | understand that | can find more information about my rights to my Health Information, and about how UofL Health uses my Health Information, in the NPP. | acknowledge that if | requested a copy of the NPP that | have been given a copy. | further understand that the NPP is available on UofL Health’s website at: www.uoflhealth.org.

| agree that my permission applies to all of my Health Information in UofL Health’s possession, including but not limited to my contact information, problem list, diagnostic test results, medication list, allergy list, immunizations, medical history, surgeries, procedures, and other clinically relevant data.

| understand that UofL Health cannot contro! how others that receive my Health Information will protect or use my information. | understand that others may not be required by law to protect my Health Information.

Purposes for which my Health Information may be shared: | understand that UofL Health may receive, use, and disclose my Health Information for the purposes outlined in the NPP. In particular, UofL Health may use and disclose my Health Information for the following purposes:

Data Sharing Program Purposes: | understand and agree that the use and disclosure of my health information for certain aspects of treatment at UofL Health may be disclosed to other people involved with my care outside UofL Health. If | desire to revoke my consent, even in an emergency situation, | must notify UofL Health’s Health Information Management Department by phone: [PHONE-2719], option 2. | understand and agree that my request will be evaluated to determine if it is reasonable and in the best interest of my health care to comply with my request. | will be notified in writing of as to whether UofL Health will be able to grant my request. If my request is granted and | am are in need of emergency treatment, UoflL Health may end the restriction if the information is needed to provide my care. (See our Notice of Privacy Practices for more information on these types of uses and disclosures for further information.)

Fundraising Activities: UofL Health relies on fundraising to help support caring for all patients. | give my permission to be contacted relating to fundraising opportunities. If | prefer not to be contacted about fundraising, | must notify UofL Health by email to: compliance @uoflhealth.org or by phone: [PHONE-2718]

Operational Purposes: | agree that the contact information | give to UofL Health, such as telephone numbers and email addresses, may be used by UofL Health and third parties acting for UofL Health to communicate with me for operational purposes including appointment reminders, follow up treatment reminders, flu shot reminders, and patient surveys.

| give my consent to UofL Health providing care and treatment, including consultation, during the course of my admission to UofL Health. By signing this consent form, | consent to each of the following:

TREATMENT

| have a condition requiring examination, diagnosis and treatment. | consent to and authorize such care including but not limited to: X-ray, laboratory, routine diagnostic tests and therapeutic procedures ("Services") performed by my admitting and treating physicians.

| acknowledge that not all health care providers including physicians, nurse anesthetists, nurse practitioners, therapists, residents and students, are employees or agents of UofL Health. Such individuals are independent contractors who bill separately for their Services. | also understand that UofL Health is not responsible for nor does it assume any liability for the acts or omissions of any independent contractor(s). | acknowledge there may be supervised participation of health care students (medical students, nursing students, interns, residents and non-physician clinical students).

| understand that my treatment may involve risks and that there are no guarantees concerning the outcome or results of this treatment and/or examination and | have the right to make decisions concerning my health care, including the right to refuse medical and surgical procedures/treatment.

TESTING FOR INFECTIOUS DISEASES

| understand that as part of my medical treatment, procedures or testing, | may be tested for Human Immunodeficiency Virus ("HIV"), Hepatitis, or any other blood borne infectious disease as well COVID-19 or any airborne pathogen if a physician or advanced practice registered nurse orders the test for diagnostic purposes. | also understand that, in the case of exposure of a health care provider or first responder (Such as an emergency worker, fire fighter or police officer) to my bodily fluids, although | will be informed of the right to consent to testing for HIV or other infectious diseases transmitted by bodily fluids, in most cases, tests may be performed on previously gathered bodily fluid samples or a court order may be obtained to compel such testing. Information concerning the fact that a test was ordered and the results of such test will remain confidential and be disclosed by UofL Health only as permitted by law.

FINANCIAL RESPONSIBILITY

| agree that | obligate myself and/or the Patient to pay the account(s) of UofL Health in accordance with the established rates and payment policy of UofL Health. | also understand that patient/guarantor payments can be applied to any account where payment is due to include any credit balance resulting from benefit payments or other sources.

| understand that if my Insurer denies all or any part of UofL Health’s charges for any reason, or if | have no insurance, | will be personally and fully responsible for payment of UofL Health’s charges. | am aware that | must notify the Business Office and submit the completed application form if | believe | may qualify for financial assistance. I/My Designated Health Care Representative authorizes billing by and direct payment to UofL Health or to the physicians providing Services at UofL Health. | understand they will bill my insurance directly for their Services, at a rate not to exceed the UofL Health’s or physician’s regular charges. Insurance includes but is not limited to, my medical health insurance policy/policies, including my employee health benefit plan, accident, worker's compensation benefits, motor vehicle insurance, casualty insurance, and uninsured or underinsured insurance

AUTHORIZATION OF REPRESENTATIVE TO APPEAL

| understand that Medicare and other payers do not pay the difference in the cost between a private and semi-private room when use of a private room is not necessary for medical reasons. | hereby agree that the difference in the cost between a semi-private and private room will be my responsibility to pay if | request one at any point during my stay.

| hereby authorize and convey to UofL Health the full extent permissible under Federal and State law and under any applicable insurance policy and/or employee health care benefit plan: (1) the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative in connection with any claim, right, matter, or cause of action that | may have under such insurance policy and/or benefit plan; and (2) the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative to pursue such claim, right, matter, or cause of action in connection with said insurance policy and/or benefit plan, including the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative with respect to a benefit plan governed by the provisions of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act ("ERISA").

| also authorize UofL Health to act on my behalf to (1) obtain copies of my insurance policy or benefit plan documents from my insurer(s) or other Plan Administrator; (2) pursue appeals of adverse benefits decisions, claims for breaches of fiduciary duty under ERISA, external appeal review under Federal or State statute, administrative hearings, or legal actions to obtain payment from any payor; (3) disclose my protected health information to my insurer(s), its affiliates, any plan fiduciary, or independent review organization that may be involved in the resolution of claims, in connection with filing or appealing any claim for benefits due; and (4) receive any notices in connection with my benefit claims or appeals concerning same.

If my admission to UofL Health is a result of a work-related injury, | hereby waive any privilege |_ may have with UofL Health, or other healthcare provider, and | hereby authorize these providers to provide the worker's compensation administrator any information related to my injury or to my past relevant medical history. In the event there is a dispute about the compensability of my claim or worker’s compensation benefits, and if my employer is not specifically determined by a Court of the Department of Labor to be responsible for worker's compensation medical expenses for the condition or injury that is the basis of my admission, | agree to be personally responsible for all such expenses. | further agree that, if my worker's compensation claim is settled with my employer on a disputed basis without a specific finding that such is compensable as a worker's compensation injury, | (or my attorney, if | am represented) will withhold sufficient funds from any settlement to pay all amounts owed to UofL Health for treatment of the condition which is the basis for this admission, and | hereby grant an assignment to UofL Health for payment of all such expenses under such circumstances.

PERSONAL EQUIPMENT AND VALUABLES

| understand that UofL Health does not accept responsibility and will not reimburse me for the loss of money, jewelry, eyewear, hearing aids, dentures, clothing or other personal property or valuables | bring to UofL Health. | take full responsibility for such items and agree to release UofL Health from any and all liability from damage, loss or theft of such items unless, as applicable, the items are deposited at UofL Health safe for safekeeping. | also understand that | must inform a nurse if | bring any electrical equipment to UofL Health (e.g., home insulin pumps; BiPAP machine, CPAP machine) and adhere to UofL Health policies regarding its use. | assume full responsibility for such electrical equipment and for any injury caused by the use of the electrical equipment brought from home.

COMMUNICATIONS

By providing my cell, landline, or any other phone number(s), | expressly consent to receiving communications from UofL Health, its staff, its contractors, collection agents, and others, at any numbers | provide or that are later acquired for me. These parties may use this information to contact me by live agent, voice mail, text message, using an auto dialer or other computer assisted technology, pre-recordedmessage(s), or by any other form of electronic communication for any purpose including, but not limited to, appointment and follow-up health care reminders, scheduling, my account(s), assignment of benefits, and/or financial responsibility. | understand that depending on my phone plan | could be charged for these calls or text messages. | agree to provide new number(s) if my number(s) change(s). Additional information: Chief Complaint from Nursing Triage Note : Chief Complaint §/4/2021 13:14 EDT Chief Complaint Right ankle pain, c/o sharp stabbing pain, MVA x2weeks ago broke both ankles .

History of Present Illness

Otherwise healthy female presents with ankle pain. On 417 patient was involved in a MVC, she was an unrestrained driver in a vehicle that was T-boned. Patient found to have bilateral ankle fractures and was taken to surgery by orthopedics with ORIF. She has follow-up scheduled on Thursday. Patient states she has had increased right ankle pain today as she ran out of her hydrocodone yesterday. She denies new injury, redness, swelling. She states ambulation or activity worsens her pain and rest improves it. She states however that hydrocodone does not completely relieve her pain. She denies chest pain, shortness of air, lower extremity edema. She is well-appearing on arrival, ambulating with a walker

Review of Systems Constitutional symptoms: No fever, no chills. Skin symptoms: No rash, Eye symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. ENMT symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. Respiratory symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. Cardiovascular symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. Gastrointestinal symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. Genitourinary symptoms: Negative except as documented in HPI. Musculoskeletal symptoms: Joint pain. Neurologic symptoms: No numbness, no tingling, no weakness.

ED Pat Edu Patient Education Materials Follows:

Closed Reduction for Ankle Fracture or Dislocation, Care After

This sheet gives you information about how to care for yourself after your procedure. Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. If you have problems or questions, contact your health care provider.

What can I expect after the procedure? After the procedure, it is common to have:

¢ Pain or discomfort.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 11 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2618] Account #: 72112451413

Emergency ¢ Swelling.

¢ Bruising or bluish discoloration.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. ¢ Ask your health care provider if the medicine prescribed to you: —46 Requires you to avoid driving or using heavy machinery. —46 Can cause constipation. You may need to take actions to prevent or treat constipation, such as: —52Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. —52Take over-the-counter or prescription medicines. —52Eat foods that are high in fiber, such as beans, whole grains, and fresh fruits and vegetables.

—52Limit foods that are high in fat and processed sugars, such as fried or sweet foods. If you have a cast, splint, or boot:

¢ Check the skin around it every day. Tell your health care provider about any concerns. * Keep it clean. ¢ Ifthe cast, splint, boot is not waterproof:

—46 Do not let it get wet.

—46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or shower. If you have a cast:

¢ Do not put pressure on any part of the cast until it is fully hardened. This may take several hours.

¢ Do not stick anything inside the cast to scratch your skin. Doing that increases your risk of infection.

¢ You may put lotion on dry skin around the edges of the cast. Do not put lotion on the skin underneath the cast.

If you have a splint or boot:

¢ Wear the splint or boot as told by your health care provider. Remove it only as told by your health care provider. ¢ Loosen the splint or boot if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 12 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2617] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

¢ If directed, put ice on your ankle area. —46 Ifyou have a removable splint or boot, remove it as told by your health care provider. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag or between your cast and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. ¢ Move your toes often to reduce stiffness and swelling.

¢ Raise (elevate) your ankle above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down. Activity

¢ Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you.

¢ Do not use the injured limb to support your body weight until your health care provider says that you can. This may take several weeks. Use crutches, a walker, or a wheelchair as told by your health care provider.

¢ Do exercises such as specific physical therapy for your ankle as instructed by your health care provider.

Driving

¢ Do not drive for 24 hours if you were given a sedative during your procedure.

¢ Ask your health care provider when it is safe to drive if you have a cast, splint, or boot on the foot that you use for driving.

General instructions

¢ Do not drink alcohol if your health care provider tells you not to drink.

¢ Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and chewing tobacco. These can delay bone healing. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 13 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2616] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if you have:

A fever.

Pain that is not controlled by your pain medicine.

Get help right away if you have:

A severe increase in pain or swelling.

Toes that tingle or become numb.

Loss of feeling in your leg, foot, or ankle.

Toes that are cold and blue.

Pain, tenderness, or redness in your calf.

A new rash around your ankle or cast, or if you notice any leakage of fluid from the skin. Bleeding around the ankle or underneath or around the cast.

Chest pain.

Difficulty breathing.

Summary

After the procedure, it is common to have pain or discomfort, swelling, and bruising or bluish discoloration around your ankle.

Follow instructions for taking care of your injury as you recover at home. You may be given specific instructions for taking medicines and wearing a boot, cast, or splint.

Use ice and medicine to control swelling and pain. Follow instructions on not bearing weight on your injured ankle. You may have to use crutches, a walker, or a wheelchair.

Contact a health care provider if you have a fever or pain that does not go away.

Get help right away if your pain worsens or your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold or blue. Also,

get help right away if you have chest pain, tenderness in your calf, a rash around your ankle or cast, or bleeding around the ankle.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Ankle Fracture

Tibia

Fibula

Talus ‘

~

C sete)

:

The ankle joint is made up of the lower (distal) sections of your lower leg bones (tibia and fibula) along with a bone in your foot (talus). An ankle fracture is a break in one, two, or all three of these sections of bone. There are two general types of ankle fractures:

¢ Stable fracture. This happens when one of your bones is broken, but the bones of your ankle joint stay in their normal positions.

¢ Unstable fracture. This type can include more than one broken bone. It can also happen if your outer bone is broken and the tough bands of tissue that connect bones (ligaments) are also injured at your inner ankle. This type of fracture allows the talus to move out of its normal position.

What are the causes? This condition may be caused by:

¢ A hard, direct hit (blow) to the ankle. * Quickly and severely twisting your ankle, often while your foot is planted and the rest of your body moving.

¢ Trauma, such as a car accident or falling from a height.

What increases the risk? This condition is more likely to occur in people who:

¢ Smoke. e Are overweight.

¢ Participate in sports that involve quick direction changes, as in soccer.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 15 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient. DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2612] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

* Do high-impact sports like gymnastics or football.

¢ Are involved in a high-impact car accident.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Symptoms of this condition include:

¢ Tender and swollen ankle.

¢ Bruising around the injured ankle.

¢ Pain when moving or pressing on the ankle.

¢ Trouble walking or using the ankle to support your body weight (putting weight on the ankle).

¢ Pain that gets worse when moving or standing and gets better with rest.

How is this diagnosed? An ankle fracture is usually diagnosed with a physical exam and X-rays. A CT scan or MRI may also be done. How is this treated?

Treatment for this condition depends on the type of ankle fracture you have. Stable fractures are treated with a cast, boot, or splint to hold the ankle still and crutches to avoid putting weight on the injured ankle until the fracture heals. Unstable fractures require surgery to ensure that the bones heal properly. After surgery, you will have a splint. After your incision is healed, your surgeon may give you a cast or a boot. You will not be able to put weight on your injured side for several weeks.

After your ankle has healed, you will do exercises to improve the strength and mobility of your ankle. Follow these instructions at home: If you have a splint:

¢ Wear the splint as told by your health care provider. Remove it only as told by your health care provider.

¢ Loosen the splint if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 16 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2611] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

¢ Keep the splint clean. ¢ Ifthe splint is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet.

—46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower. If you have a cast:

¢ Do not stick anything inside the cast to scratch your skin. Doing that increases your risk of infection.

¢ Check the skin around the cast every day. Tell your health care provider about any concerns.

¢ You may put lotion on dry skin around the edges of the cast. Do not put lotion on the skin underneath the cast.

* Keep the cast clean.

¢ Ifthe cast is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet.

—46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower.

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

¢ If directed, put ice on the injured area: —46 Ifyou have a removable splint, remove it as told by your health care provider. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag or between your cast and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

¢ Move your toes often to avoid stiffness and to lessen swelling.

* Raise (elevate) the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down.

General instructions

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 17 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2610] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

¢ Do not use the injured limb to support your body weight until your health care provider says that you can. Use crutches as told by your health care provider ¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

¢ Ask your health care provider when it is safe to drive if you have a cast or splint. ¢ Do exercises as told by your health care provider.

* Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. These can delay bone healing. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider ¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if: ¢ You have pain or swelling that gets worse or does not get better with rest or medicine. Get help right away if:

¢ Your cast gets damaged. ¢ You have severe pain that lasts. ¢ You develop new pain or swelling.

¢ Your skin or toenails below the injury turn blue or gray, feel cold, become numb, or have a loss of sensitivity to touch.

Summary

e An ankle fracture can either be stable or unstable. This is determined after a physical exam and imaging studies like X-rays, a CT scan, or MRI.

¢ Stable fractures are treated with a cast, boot, or splint to hold the ankle still until the fracture heals. Unstable fractures require surgery to ensure that the bones heal properly.

¢ You will not be able to put weight on your injured side for several weeks.

¢ Pain medicines, icing, and raising (elevating) your injured ankle when sitting or lying down may help with pain relief. Follow instructions as told by your health care provider.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

¢ If directed, put ice on the injured area: —46 Ifyou have a removable splint, remove it as told by your health care provider. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag or between your cast and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

¢ Move your toes often to avoid stiffness and to lessen swelling.

Contact a health care provider if: ¢ You have pain or swelling that gets worse or does not get better with rest or medicine. Get help right away if:

¢ Your skin or toenails below the injury turn blue or gray, feel cold, become numb, or have a loss of sensitivity to touch.

Summary

e An ankle fracture can either be stable or unstable. This is determined after a physical exam and imaging studies like X-rays, a CT scan, or MRI.

¢ Stable fractures are treated with a cast, boot, or splint to hold the ankle still until the fracture heals. Unstable fractures require surgery to ensure that the bones heal properly.

¢ You will not be able to put weight on your injured side for several weeks.

¢ Pain medicines, icing, and raising (elevating) your injured ankle when sitting or lying down may help with pain relief. Follow instructions as told by your health care provider.

This sheet gives you information about how to care for yourself after your procedure. Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. If you have problems or questions, contact your health care provider.

What can I expect after the procedure? After the procedure, it is common to have:

¢ Pain or discomfort. ¢ Swelling.

¢ Bruising or bluish discoloration.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. ¢ Ask your health care provider if the medicine prescribed to you: —46 Requires you to avoid driving or using heavy machinery. —46 Can cause constipation. You may need to take actions to prevent or treat constipation, such as: —52Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. —52Take over-the-counter or prescription medicines. —52Eat foods that are high in fiber, such as beans, whole grains, and fresh fruits and vegetables.

—52Limit foods that are high in fat and processed sugars, such as fried or sweet foods.

If you have a cast, splint, or boot:

¢ Check the skin around it every day. Tell your health care provider about any concerns. * Keep it clean. ¢ Ifthe cast, splint, boot is not waterproof:

—46 Do not let it get wet.

If you have a splint or boot:

As part of your treatment plan, you provider may have prescribed a limited course of a controlled substance. This medication may be given to help people with moderate or severe pain or for other medical conditions, but there are risks involved with treatment. Common side effects may include nausea, constipation, drowsiness, sweating, itching, dry mouth, and rash. More serious side effects may include cognitive and motor impairment, like problems with thinking, concentrating, alertness, and movement (e.g. slowed reflexes), and driving and operating heavy machinery can be dangerous. It is important for you to talk to your provider if you have these side effects or questions. These controlled substances can produce physical dependence and be habit-forming if taken for an extended period of time, which means that the body has gotten used o them and may experience withdrawal symptoms, if they are abruptly stopped. Withdrawal symptoms can include runny nose, sweating, goose bumps, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, rapid heartbeat, difficulty sleeping, and nervousness. Please dispose of unused and expired medications per pharmacy guidance.

Ankle Fracture

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 31 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2580] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

Tibia Fibula

Talus

The ankle joint is made up of the lower (distal) sections of your lower leg bones (tibia and fibula) along with a bone in your foot (talus). An ankle fracture is a break in one, two, or all three of these sections of bone. There are two general types of ankle fractures:

. Stable fracture. This happens when one of your bones is broken, but the bones of your ankle joint stay in their normal positions.

° Unstable fracture. This type can include more than one broken bone. It can also happen if your outer bone is broken and the tough bands of tissue that connect bones (ligaments) are also injured at your inner ankle. This type of fracture allows the talus to move out of its normal position.

What are the causes? This condition may be caused by:

° A hard, direct hit (blow) to the ankle. ° Quickly and severely twisting your ankle, often while your foot is planted and the rest of your body moving. . Trauma, such as a car accident or falling from a height.

What increases the risk? This condition is more likely to occur in people who:

° Smoke.

° Are overweight.

° Participate in sports that involve quick direction changes, as in soccer. ° Do high-impact sports like gymnastics or football.

. Are involved in a high-impact car accident.

What are the signs or symptoms?

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 32 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient. DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2579] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

Symptoms of this condition include:

. Tender and swollen ankle.

° Bruising around the injured ankle.

° Pain when moving or pressing on the ankle.

. Trouble walking or using the ankle to support your body weight (putting weight on the ankle). ° Pain that gets worse when moving or standing and gets better with rest.

How is this diagnosed?

An ankle fracture is usually diagnosed with a physical exam and X-rays. A CT scan or MRI may also be done.

How is this treated?

Treatment for this condition depends on the type of ankle fracture you have. Stable fractures are treated with a cast, boot, or splint to hold the ankle still and crutches to avoid putting weight on the injured ankle until the fracture heals. Unstable fractures require surgery to ensure that the bones heal properly. After surgery, you will have a splint. After your incision is healed, your surgeon may give you a cast or a boot. You will not be able to put weight on your injured side for several weeks.

After your ankle has healed, you will do exercises to improve the strength and mobility of your ankle.

Follow these instructions at home: If you have a splint:

° Wear the splint as told by your health care provider. Remove it only as told by your health care provider. ° Loosen the splint if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue. ° Keep the splint clean. ° If the splint is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet. —46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower.

If you have a cast:

° Do not stick anything inside the cast to scratch your skin. Doing that increases your risk of infection.

° Check the skin around the cast every day. Tell your health care provider about any concerns.

° You may put lotion on dry skin around the edges of the cast. Do not put lotion on the skin underneath the cast. ° Keep the cast clean.

° If the cast is not waterproof:

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

Move your toes often to avoid stiffness and to lessen swelling. . Raise (elevate) the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down.

General instructions

Do not use the injured limb to support your body weight until your health care provider says that you can. Use crutches as told by your health care provider

Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

Ask your health care provider when it is safe to drive if you have a cast or splint.

You have pain or swelling that gets worse or does not get better with rest or medicine. Get help right away if:

° Your cast gets damaged. ° You have severe pain that lasts. . You develop new pain or swelling.

Your skin or toenails below the injury turn blue or gray, feel cold, become numb, or have a loss of sensitivity to touch.

Summary

An ankle fracture can either be stable or unstable. This is determined after a physical exam and imaging studies like X-rays, a CT scan, or MRI.

Document Revised: [DOB-367] Document Reviewed: [DOB-368] Elsevier Patient Education © 2020 Elsevier Inc.

Emergency Awareness and Preventative Care

STROKE is an EMERGENCY Every Minute Counts

Act FAST and Check for these signs:

FACE Does the face look uneven? ARM Does one arm drift down?

Does their speech sound SPEECH strange? TIME Call 9-1-1 at any sign of stroke Stroke Risk Factors Atrial Fibrillation . Family history of (irregular heartbeat) Diabetes stroke Heart Disease Heavy alcohol use High Blood Pressure

Physical inactivity

High Cholesterol 9 and obesity

Smoking

Cigarette Smoking

The facts are clear, cigarette smoking will shorten your life. Smoking can cause many illnesses along the way. As a healthcare provider, we recommend that you stop smoking. Assistance with quitting is available by contacting 1-800-QUIT-NOW. This is a free resource providing counseling, support, and referral. Or you may contact your personal physician.

National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 1-[PHONE-2574]

The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline is a national network of local crisis centers that provides free and confidential emotional support to people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

Don't Wait! Stop a Heart Attack Before It Starts

What is a heart attack?

A heart attack is damage or death to a part of the heart from severely decreased or lack of blood flow to the heart. Over time, arteries can become narrow from the buildup of fat and cholesterol, which is called plaque. The plaque can rupture causing a blood clot to form. When the blood clot forms, the artery can become severely narrowed or completely blocked, causing a heart attack.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290761 Page 35 of 97 12/3/2021 12:57 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2573] Account #: 72112451413 Emergency

¢ Heart attack is the leading cause of death in the United States. * 85% of muscle damage occurs within the first 2 hours.

* Delay in the recognition of heart attack symptoms increases the chances of death.

Know the early symptoms of a heart attack:

Feeling of Pain that travels Nausea "fullness" in Jaw Pain down one or chest both arms . . Chest pressure, Fatigue/being Anxiety Back Pain squeezing, or discomfort Shortness of Sweating, or a A ecling oF breath cold sweat impending doom

There are unusual signs of heart attack, too! Women, the elderly, and diabetics may present with atypical symptoms:

* Fainting/dizziness ¢ Weakness

* Confusion

Risk Factors for a Heart Attack

Some heart disease risk factors, such as age and family, cannot be changed. Others, like smoking and lack of exercise, can be changed.

Smoking High Cholesterol Blood Pressure Family History Obesity Age

Gender (Males are at . .

higher risk) Lack of Exercise Diabetes

Diet Stress Excessive Alcohol

Intake

If you or someone you know is experiencing the signs and symptoms of a heart attack, DON'T DELAY. Call 9-1-1 immediately and seek help.

If someone collapses, perform CPR!

Do not attempt to drive if you are having symptoms of heart attack.

Why Hands-Only CPR?

* Hands-Only CPR has been shown to be as effective as conventional CPR for cardiac arrests that occur outside of a hospital.

* Survival depends on immediately receiving CPR from someone nearby.

How do you perform Hands-Only CPR? There are two easy steps:

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE MRN: [PHONE-2572]

* 1. Call 9-1-1 if you see a teen or adult collapse

DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female Account #: T[PHONE-2571] Emergency

¢ 2. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest at a beat of 100 beats per minute

Save a life!

4 WAYS TO GET AHEAD OF SEPSIS

SEPSIS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Time Matters!

Infections put you and your family at risk for a life-threatening condition called sepsis.

* Sepsis is the body's extreme response to an infection. It is life-threatening, and without timely treatment, sepsis can rapidly lead to tissue damage, organ failure, and death. Sepsis happens when an infection you already have-in your skin, lungs, urinary tract or somewhere else-riggers a chain reaction throughout your body.

PREVENT INFECTIONS

PRACTICE GOOD HYGIENE

KNOW THE SYMPTOMS

ACT FAST

EGG

Your Records

Take good care of chronic conditions. Talk to your doctor about getting the recommended vaccines.

Wash your hands frequently.

Keep cuts or open sores clean and covered until they

are healed.

Confusion or disorientation Shortness of breath High heart rate Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold Extreme pain or discomfort Clammy or sweaty skin

Get medical care IMMEDIATELY if

you suspect sepsis or if you have an

infection that is not getting better or is getting worse.

To learn more about sepsis and how to prevent infections, visit www.cdc.gov/sepsis.

The examination and treatment you have received in the Emergency Department has been done to provide an appropriate evaluation and stabilizing treatment on an emregency basis only. Given the limited resources, it is not meant to be a substitute for complete medical care. The follow-up provider you named will receive a copy of your records and all test reports. IT IS IMPORTANT THAT YOU SCHEDULE A FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENT AND RE-EVALUATED. You should report any new complaints, symptoms, or remaining problems at that time. IT IS IMPOSSIBLE FOR THE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT TO RECOGNIZE AND TREAT ALL ELEMENTS OF INJURY OR ILLNESS IN A SINGLE VISIT.

If you have been referred to a specialist provider, it means that we believe you may have a condition that requires the expertise of a specialist. These providers work in partnership with the hospital and have agreed to see referred patients in their office for further evaluation. KEEP IN MIND THAT THE SPECIALIST HAS HIS/HER OWN OFFICE POLICIES WHICH MAY REQUIRE PROPER INSURANCE OR PAYMENT UP FRONT BEFORE THE SPECIALIST WILL SEE YOU. It is your responsibility to call the specialist provider to make an appointment. We do not have the ability to refer patients to specialists/physicians that work with specific insurance companies.

Please be advised that all financial charges or billing practices are determined by that practice, not the hospital. If your insurance company requires that you see a specialist from their approved list, it is your responsibility to contact your insurance company to make those arrangements. It is also your responsibility to follow any other requirements of your insurance company necessary to obtain coverage for claims submitted.
