Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-446] Account #: T[PHONE-447] Diagnostic Radiology Accession Exam STATUS Exam Date/Time Ordering Provider 40-CR-21-0039822 CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT Auth (Verified) 5/4/2021 14:23 EDT ZELLER,ANGELIA,NP- INT

Reason For Exam (CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT) recent orif with new worse pain

Report

EXAM: CR Ankle Min 3 Vws RT

ACCESSION NUMBER: 40CR210039822

DATE: [DOB-2495] 2:06 PM

PROVIDED INDICATION: recent orif with new worse pain COMPARISON: Ankle radiographs [DOB-449]

TECHNIQUE: AP lateral, and oblique radiographs of the right ankle.

FINDINGS:

EXAM QUALITY: Adequate for interpretation.

BONES: Again seen are 2 cannulated retrograde fixation screws traversing through the medial malleolar fracture fragment into the distal tibia. The medial malleolar fracture fragment appears in near anatomic alignment and positioning. No evidence of new fracture. No lucencies associated with the hardware or discontinuity in the hardware to suggest infection or hardware failure.

JOINTS: Joints are in normal alignment.

SOFT TISSUES: No radiopaque foreign body is seen in the soft tissues.

OTHER: Overlying external brace.

IMPRESSION: Near anatomic alignment and positioning of the previously noted medial malleolar fracture. No evidence of new fracture and/or dislocation.

"|, the attending/teaching physician, have personally reviewed, discussed, and supervised this radiological examination with the resident and this report reflects my agreement."

AUTHORIZATION OF REPRESENTATIVE TO APPEAL

| understand that Medicare and other payers do not pay the difference in the cost between a private and semi-private room when use of a private room is not necessary for medical reasons. | hereby agree that the difference in the cost between a semi-private and private room will be my responsibility to pay if | request one at any point during my stay.

| hereby authorize and convey to UofL Health the full extent permissible under Federal and State law and under any applicable insurance policy and/or employee health care benefit plan: (1) the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative in connection with any claim, right, matter, or cause of action that | may have under such insurance policy and/or benefit plan; and (2) the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative to pursue such claim, right, matter, or cause of action in connection with said insurance policy and/or benefit plan, including the right and ability to act as my Authorized Representative with respect to a benefit plan governed by the provisions of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act ("ERISA").

| also authorize UofL Health to act on my behalf to (1) obtain copies of my insurance policy or benefit plan documents from my insurer(s) or other Plan Administrator; (2) pursue appeals of adverse benefits decisions, claims for breaches of fiduciary duty under ERISA, external appeal review under Federal or State statute, administrative hearings, or legal actions to obtain payment from any payor; (3) disclose my protected health information to my insurer(s), its affiliates, any plan fiduciary, or independent review organization that may be involved in the resolution of claims, in connection with filing or appealing any claim for benefits due; and (4) receive any notices in connection with my benefit claims or appeals concerning same.

|f my admission to UofL Health is a result of a work-related injury, | hereby waive any privilege | may have with UofL Health, or other healthcare provider, and | hereby authorize these providers to provide the worker’s compensation administrator any information related to my injury or to my past relevant medical history. In the event there is a dispute about the compensability of my claim or worker's compensation benefits, and if my employer is not specifically determined by a Court of the Department of Labor to be responsible for worker's compensation medical expenses for the condition or injury that is the basis of my admission, | agree to be personally responsible for all such expenses. | further agree that, if my worker's compensation claim is settled with my employer on a disputed basis without a specific finding that such is compensable as a worker's compensation injury, | (or my attorney, if | am represented) will withhold sufficient funds from any settlement to pay all amounts owed to UofL Health for treatment of the condition which is the basis for this admission, and | hereby grant an assignment to UofL Health for payment of all such expenses under such circumstances.

PERSONAL EQUIPMENT AND VALUABLES

| understand that UofL Health does not accept responsibility and will not reimburse me for the loss of money, jewelry, eyewear, hearing aids, dentures, clothing or other personal property or valuables | bring to UofL Health. | take full responsibility for such items and agree to release UofL Health from any and all liability from damage, loss or theft of such items unless, as applicable, the items are deposited at UofL Health safe for safekeeping. | also understand that | must inform a nurse if | bring any electrical equipment to UofL Health (e.g., home insulin pumps; BIPAP machine, CPAP machine) and adhere to UofL Health policies regarding its use. | assume full responsibility for such electrical equipment and for any injury caused by the use of the electrical equipment brought from home.

COMMUNICATIONS

By providing my cell, landline, or any other phone number(s), | expressly consent to receiving communications from UofL Health, its staff, its contractors, collection agents, and others, at any numbers | provide or that are later acquired for me. These parties may use this information to contact me by live agent, voice mail, text message, using an auto dialer or other computer assisted technology, pre-recorded message(s), or by any other form of electronic communication for any purpose including, but not limited to, appointment and follow-up health care reminders, scheduling, my account(s), assignment of benefits, and/or financial responsibility. | understand that depending on my phone plan | could be charged for these calls or text messages. | agree to provide new number(s) if my number(s) change(s). Providing these numbers is not a condition of receiving healthcare services.

The term “UofL Health” means UofL Health at any of its hospitals, offices, and/or other locations as well as all of University of Louisville Physicians, nurses, and other staff, employees or affiliates.

| give my consent to UofL Health providing care and treatment, including consultation, during the course of my admission to UofL Health. By signing this consent form, | consent to each of the following:

TREATMENT

| have a condition requiring examination, diagnosis and treatment. | consent to and authorize such care including but not limited to: X-ray, laboratory, routine diagnostic tests and therapeutic procedures ("Services") performed by my admitting and treating physicians.

| acknowledge that not all health care providers including physicians, nurse anesthetists, nurse practitioners, therapists, residents and students, are employees or agents of UofL Health. Such individuals are independent contractors who bill separately for their Services. | also understand that UofL Health is not responsible for nor does it assume any liability for the acts or omissions of any independent contractor(s). | acknowledge there may be supervised participation of health care students (medical students, nursing students, interns, residents and non-physician clinical students).

| understand that my treatment may involve risks and that there are no guarantees concerning the outcome or results of this treatment and/or examination and | have the right to make decisions concerning my health care, including the right to refuse medical and surgical procedures/treatment.

TESTING FOR INFECTIOUS DISEASES

| understand that as part of my medical treatment, procedures or testing, | may be tested for Human Immunodeficiency Virus ("HIV"), Hepatitis, or any other blood borne infectious disease as well COVID-19 or any airborne pathogen if a physician or advanced practice registered nurse orders the test for diagnostic purposes. | also understand that, in the case of exposure of a health care provider or first responder (such as an emergency worker, fire fighter or police officer) to my bodily fluids, although | will be informed of the right to consent to testing for HIV or other infectious diseases transmitted by bodily fluids, in most cases, tests may be performed on previously gathered bodily fluid samples or a court order may be obtained to compel such testing. Information concerning the fact that a test was ordered and the results of such test will remain confidential and be disclosed by UofL Health only as permitted by law.

FINANCIAL RESPONSIBILITY

| agree that | obligate myself and/or the Patient to pay the account(s) of UoflL Health in accordance with the established rates and payment policy of UofL Health. | also understand that patient/guarantor payments can be applied to any account where payment is due to include any credit balance resulting from benefit payments or other sources.

| understand that if my Insurer denies all or any part of UofL Health's charges for any reason, or if | have no insurance, | will be personally and fully responsible for payment of UofL Health’s charges. | am aware that | must notify the Business Office and submit the completed application form if | believe | may qualify for financial assistance. I/My Designated Health Care Representative authorizes billing by and direct payment to UofL Health or to the physicians providing Services at UofL Health. | understand they will bill my insurance directly for their Services, at a rate not to exceed the UofL Health’s or physician’s regular charges. Insurance includes but is not limited to, my medical health insurance policy/policies, including my employee health benefit plan, accident, worker's compensation benefits, motor vehicle insurance, casualty insurance, and uninsured or underinsured insurance

USING AND SHARING MY INFORMATION

The Law: There is a federal law called the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (‘HIPAA’). This law requires UofL Health to protect the privacy and security of its patients’ treatment, contact, and financial information. Taken together, this information is called your “Health Information”. There are also other federal and/or state laws that require UofL Health to take additional steps to protect certain categories of Health Information, including, but not limited to, Health Information about behavioral or mental health; developmental disabilities; treatment for substance abuse (alcohol and/or drugs) disorders; genetic testing and counseling; HIV/AIDS; sexual assault/ abuse; sexually transmitted illnesses; pregnancy; birth control; domestic abuse of an adult with a disability; child abuse and neglect.

Authorization and Notice of Privacy Practices (“NPP”):|f my permission is required by law, by signing this form | agree that UofL Health may receive, use and disclose my Health Information as set forth in this Consent and as set forth in UofL Health’s NPP. | understand that | can find more information about my rights to my Health Information, and about how UofL Health uses my Health Information, in the NPP. | acknowledge that if | requested a copy of the NPP that | have been given a copy. | further understand that the NPP is available on UofL Health's website at: www.uoflhealth.org.

| agree that my permission applies to all of my Health Information in UofL Health's possession, including but not limited to my contact information, problem list, diagnostic test results, medication list, allergy list, immunizations, medical history, surgeries, procedures, and other clinically relevant data.

| understand that UofL Health cannot control how others that receive my Health Information will protect or use my information. | understand that others may not be required by law to protect my Health Information.

Purposes for which my Health Information may be shared: | understand that UofL Health may receive, use, and disclose my Health Information for the purposes outlined in the NPP. In particular, UofL Health may use and disclose my Health Information for the following purposes:

Data Sharing Program Purposes: | understand and agree that the use and disclosure of my health information for certain aspects of treatment at UofL Health may be disclosed to other people involved with my care outside UofL Health. If | desire to revoke my consent, even in an emergency situation, | must notify UofL Health’s Health Information Management Department by phone: [PHONE-466], option 2. | understand and agree that my request will be evaluated to determine if it is reasonable and in the best interest of my health care to comply with my request. | will be notified in writing of as to whether UofL Health will be able to grant my request. If my request is granted and | am are in need of emergency treatment, Uofl Health may end the restriction if the information is needed to provide my care. (See our Notice of Privacy Practices for more information on these types of uses and disclosures for further information.)

Fundraising Activities: Uofl Health relies on fundraising to help support caring for all patients. | give my permission to be contacted relating to fundraising opportunities. If | prefer not to be contacted about fundraising, | must notify UofL Health by email to: [EMAIL-467] or by phone: [PHONE-468]

Discharge Follow Up U of L Orthopedics - [DOB-526] 08:40 - Dr. Hartley

Code Status Full Code - Start: 04/17/21 8:38:00 EDT, Full Code

Brief Admission HPI/Presentation:

Chief Complaint Unrestrained passenger involved in MVA with airbag deployment and windsheild starring. C/O L ankle, bilateral wrist pain. History of Present Illness

22 year old female that was an unrestrained passenger in a motor vehicle accident. Another driver ran a red light and struck her vehicle. She is having pain in both ankles. She did not ambulate after the accident. The pain is moderate, worse with movement, improved with immobilization.

PMH: None

PSH: None

SH: Lives in Louisville, works at a gas station, single, smokes 1 black and mild daily, drinks alcohol socially, smokes marijuana occasionally [1]

Hospital Course

_A 22-year-old female brought to University of

Louisville Hospital by ambulance ALS on 4—17—2021 to the trauma room 9 after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. Patient was a unrestrained passenger in the vehicle she was in was T-boned on the driver side. Patient denies any LOC. Patient is reporting positive airbag deployment. Patient is reporting bilateral ankle pain and wrist pain. Denies any past medical or surgical history. The patient has been seen and evaluated by the emergency room physicians for other injuries including head, neck, chest, spine, abdomen, vascular systems and deemed hemodynamic stable. In the ED trauma 9 patient

received procedural sedation twice to reduce the bilateral ankle fractures. The ED also placed the patient bilateral lower extremity splints. Patient is nonweightbearing to bilateral extremity. Orthopedics was consulted for right medial malleolus fracture and left tibiotalar dislocation. Patient will be admitted to chief orthopedic team with Dr. Hartley as attending. Patient will continue be nonweightbearing to bilateral lower extremities. Patient will n.p.o. will be on IV fluids. Patient started medication pain control bowel program, calcium vitamin D supplementation DVT prophylaxis. The patient went to the operating room on 4-18-21 in which the left ankle was evaluated under anesthesia for closed treatment of left tibiotalar dislocation with manipulation and determined to be stable. The patient had a open reduction and internal fixation of the right medial malleolus fracture. In the operating room after surgery patient was placed in left and right lower extremity Cam walking boot. Patient will weightbearing as tolerated to bilateral lower extremities in cam walking boot. Patient

COMPARISON: Left ankle radiograph [DOB-528], CT left lower extremity [DOB-529].TECHNIQUE: AP, lateral, and mortise views of the left ankle were obtained.FINDINGS:There has been closed reduction of the comminuted fracture of the posterior talar process, with fracture involving the posteromedial and anteromedial talus, which is better appreciated compared to prior radiographs, and appears only minimally displaced with small medial fragments medially displaced. Fractures are stabilized by overlying brace, the metal components of which completely obscure the left ankle on lateral view. The ankle mortise otherwise appears intact. No additional fracture is identified. There is diffuse superficial soft tissue swelling about the left ankle. |IMPRESSION:1. Fracture is further stabilized by overlying brace, the metal components of which obscure the ankle joint on lateral view. Mortise view is limited due to difficult patient positioning. Within this limitation, the ankle mortise otherwise appears intact. Addendum by HARTLEY, BRANDI, MD-ORT on April 17, 2021 10:44:19 EDT

Pt seen and examined pre-operatively with no family present. Pertinent laboratory results, radiographic and advanced imaging reports reviewed. Pertinent radiographs and advanced imaging also viewed and reviewed by me personally. Agree with above with the following additions/changes:

HPI: + bilateral ankle pain, sharp, 10/10 at its worst, constant, better with pain medication, worse with movement, present since time of injury.

Ros:

Constitutional: No fevers or chills

Cardiovascular: No chest pain

ENT: No hearing problems

Respiratory: No shortness of breath

Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain

Musculoskeletal: + bilateral ankle pain

Skin: + scrapes and bruises

Neurological: No acute numbness, tingling or weakness Psychiatric: Anxious about current injuries Heme/Lymphatic: No history of bleeding or clotting problems

Past medical history: as above

Past surgical history: as above

Social history: as above, + smoker Family history: No bleeds or clots known

Exam:

Vitals as above

Cardiovascular: Bilateral radial and PT pulses palpable

Lymphatic: None palpable about the extremities

Skin: Visible skin intact in the RUE, LUE, RLE, LLE

Neuro: Coordination intact, Patellar tendon reflexes not tested due to injury. Sensation intact to light tough RUE, LUE, RLE, LLE, normal mood and affect, alert and oriented to person, place, time and events.

Constitutional: Well developed, well nourished. Vitals as above

MSK:

As above

No signs of DVT, PE, or compartment syndrome.

AP:

1. Right medial malleolus fracture: will require surgical stabilization to restore length, alignment, and function. Will require postoperative pain control and physical therapy. Discussed increased risk of PTOA due to intra-articular nature of the fracture.

2. Left tibiotalar dislocation with small avulsion fractures of the talus: will require EUA of the ankle stability. If unstable, external fixation will be required; however, if stable, amenable to nonoperative management in a CAM boot, the avulsion fractures require no surgical intervention. Discussed increased risk of PTOA due to dislocation and cartilaginous injury along with AVN.

2. Risks, benefits and alternatives of operative vs non-operative treatment discussed extensively. Specific risks discussed included but were not limited to: blood loss requiring transfusion, infection/osteomyelitis possibly requiring multiple future surgeries, malunion/nonunion, the possibility of future surgeries, chronic pain, disability, damage to nerves, tendons or blood vessels, DVT/PE, and death. All questions answered. No guarantees given.

247a dispatched to mva, on scene, pt was found on side walk with friend, pt involved in mva with multiple air bags deployed side and front, staring on the windshield, pt wasnt wearing seatbelt, dashboard was intact, pt was in passengers seat, pt denied head, neck, chest pain, pt complained of left ankle pain, pt appears to be very anxious, pt screaming and yelling about ever 5 mins that she was in a car accident, pt distracted and talking on the phone, had a difficult time getting pt information, pt was lifted with fires help to stretcher, left leg elevated for ankle deformity/swelling, pt secured to stretcher via seatbelts/side rails and safely loaded into ambulance, blanket placed around ankle to help immobilize with ice pack place on top, pt continued to scream and yell, pt in lots of pain and agitated, pt head to toe trauma assessed, possible injuries as noted left ankle and left wrist not able to move well, pt then moved both wrists and stated "my wrists hurt too", no other trauma injuries noted, pt transported non emergent to er, upon arrival to er no changes en route, pt wheeled in on stretcher, pt moved from stretcher to bed via bed sheet pull, report and transfer of care given to rn without change in status.

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2291] Account #: T[PHONE-2290] Emergency DOCUMENT TYPE: ED Pat Edu RESULT STATUS: Auth (Verified) SIGNED INFORMATION: HEIL,CYNTHIA,RN (4/19/2021 16:53 EDT) SERVICE DATE/TIME 4/19/2021 16:53 EDT ED Pat Edu

Patient Education Materials Follows:

Motor Vehicle Collision Injury, Adult

After a car accident (motor vehicle collision), it is common to have injuries to your head, face, arms, and body. These injuries may include:

¢ Cuts. ¢ Burns. ¢ Bruises.

¢ Sore muscles or a stretch or tear in a muscle (strain).

¢ Headaches.

You may feel stiff and sore for the first several hours. You may feel worse after waking up the first morning after the accident. These injuries often feel worse for the first 24-48 hours. After that, you will usually begin to get better with each day. How quickly you get better often depends on:

* How bad the accident was.

¢ How many injuries you have.

¢ Where your injuries are.

¢ What types of injuries you have. ¢ Ifyou were wearing a seat belt.

¢ Ifyour airbag was used.

A head injury may result in a concussion. This is a type of brain injury that can have serious effects. If you have a concussion, you should rest as told by your doctor. You must be very careful to avoid having a second concussion.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 38 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient. DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2289] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ If you were prescribed antibiotic medicine, take or apply it as told by your doctor. Do not stop using the antibiotic even if your condition gets better.

If you have a wound or a burn:

Normal Infected wound wound

¢ Clean your wound or burn as told by your doctor. —46 Wash it with mild soap and water. —46_ Rinse it with water to get all the soap off. —46 Pat it dry with a clean towel. Do not rub it. —46 Ifyou were told to put an ointment or cream on the wound, do so as told by your doctor. ¢ Follow instructions from your doctor about how to take care of your wound or burn. Make sure you: —46 Know when and how to change or remove your bandage (dressing). —46 Always wash your hands with soap and water before and after you change your bandage. If you cannot use soap and water, use hand sanitizer. —46 Leave stitches (sutures), skin glue, or skin tape (adhesive) strips in place, if you have these. They may need to stay in place for 2 weeks or longer. If tape strips get loose and curl up, you may trim the loose edges. Do not remove tape strips completely unless your doctor says it is okay. * Do not: —46 Scratch or pick at the wound or burn. —46 Break any blisters you may have. —46 Peel any skin. ¢ Avoid getting sun on your wound or burn. ¢ Raise (elevate) the wound or burn above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down. If

you have a wound or burn on your face, you may want to sleep with your head raised. You may do this by putting an extra pillow under your head.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 39 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2288] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ Check your wound or burn every day for signs of infection. Check for: —46 More redness, swelling, or pain. —46 More fluid or blood. —46 Warmth. —46 Pus ora bad smell.

Activity

¢ Rest. Rest helps your body to heal. Make sure you: —46 Get plenty of sleep at night. Avoid staying up late. —46 Go to bed at the same time on weekends and weekdays.

¢ Ask your doctor if you have any limits to what you can lift.

¢ Ask your doctor when you can drive, ride a bicycle, or use heavy machinery. Do not do these activities if you are dizzy.

¢ If you are told to wear a brace on an injured arm, leg, or other part of your body, follow instructions

from your doctor about activities. Your doctor may give you instructions about driving, bathing, exercising, or working.

General instructions

Pale yellow

¢ Iftold, put ice on the injured areas. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. ¢ Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) pale yellow.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 40 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2287] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ Do not drink alcohol. ¢ Eat healthy foods.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Contact a doctor if:

¢ Your symptoms get worse. ¢ You have neck pain that gets worse or has not improved after 1 week. ¢ You have signs of infection in a wound or burn. * You have a fever. * You have any of the following symptoms for more than 2 weeks after your car accident: —46 Lasting (chronic) headaches. —46 Dizziness or balance problems. —46 Feeling sick to your stomach (nauseous). —46 Problems with how you see (vision). —46 More sensitivity to noise or light. —46 Depression or mood swings. —46 Feeling worried or nervous (anxiety). —46 Getting upset or bothered easily. —46 Memory problems. —46 Trouble concentrating or paying attention. —46 Sleep problems. —46 Feeling tired all the time.

Get help right away if:

¢ You have: —46 Loss of feeling (numbness), tingling, or weakness in your arms or legs. —46 Very bad neck pain, especially tenderness in the middle of the back of your neck. —46 A change in your ability to control your pee or poop (stool). —46 More pain in any area of your body. —46 Swelling in any area of your body, especially your legs. —46 Shortness of breath or light-headedness.

—46 Chest pain. —46 Blood in your pee, poop, or vomit. —46_ Very bad pain in your belly (abdomen) or your back. —46 Very bad headaches or headaches that are getting worse. —46 Sudden vision loss or double vision.

¢ Your eye suddenly turns red.

¢ The black center of your eye (pupil) is an odd shape or size. Summary

e After a car accident (motor vehicle collision), it is common to have injuries to your head, face, arms, and body.

¢ Follow instructions from your doctor about how to take care of a wound or burn.

¢ Iftold, put ice on your injured areas.

¢ Contact a doctor if your symptoms get worse.

Incision Care An incision (cut) is when a surgeon cuts into your body. After surgery, the cut needs to be well cared for to keep it from getting infected.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 42 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

GSEIF & ASSOCIATES. INC. 2HRS

HOW TO CARE FOR YOUR CUT ¢ Take medicines only as told by your doctor. ¢ There are many different ways to close and cover a cut, including stitches, skin glue, and adhesive strips.

Follow your doctor's instructions on: Remove the right leg post-operative dressing on Friday, 4/23/21. Then, begin to clean the incisions with antibacterial soap and water and rinse and pat dry. If needed due to drainage or skin irritation or CAM boot wear then cover with a dry dressings changing daily and as needed. —Gare of the cut.

—Bandage (dressing) changes and removal.

—Gut closure removal.

* Do not take baths, swim, or use a hot tub until your doctor says it is okay. You may shower as told by your doctor.

¢ Return to your normal diet and activities as allowed by your doctor.

¢ Use medicine that helps lessen itching on your cut as told by your doctor. Do not pick or scratch at your cut. ¢ Drink enough fluids to keep your pee (urine) clear or pale yellow.

GET HELP IF:

¢ You have redness, puffiness (swelling), or pain at the site of your cut.

¢ You have fluid, blood, or pus coming from your cut.

¢ Your muscles ache.

¢ You have chills or you feel sick.

¢ You have a bad smell coming from the cut or bandage.

¢ Your cut opens up after stitches, staples, or adhesive strips have been removed.

¢ You keep feeling sick to your stomach (nauseous) or keep throwing up (vomiting). * You have a fever.

¢ You are dizzy.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Venous Thromboembolism Prevention

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) is a condition in which a blood clot (thrombus) develops in the body. A deep vein thrombosis (DVT) 1s a blood clot that usually occurs in a deep vein in the leg or the pelvis, but it can also occur in the arm.

Sometimes, pieces of a thrombus can break off and travel through the bloodstream to other parts of the body. When that happens, the thrombus is called an embolus. An embolus that travels to the lungs is called a pulmonary embolism. An embolism can block the blood flow in the blood vessels of other organs as well.

How can this condition affect me?

VTE 1s a serious health condition that can cause disability or death. It is very important to get help right away. Do not ignore your symptoms.

What can increase my risk? You are more likely to develop this condition if you:

¢ Have had recent major surgery or trauma.

¢ Have certain health conditions, such as cancer.

¢ Are in the hospital for a sudden illness.

¢ Have not moved for a long period of time, such as if you are on bed rest or traveling a long distance. ¢ Take certain medicines, such as birth control pills or hormone replacement therapy.

¢ Are pregnant or recently gave birth.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 44 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2278] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ Have a personal or family history of VTE. e Are overweight. ¢ Are 60 years of age or older.

¢ Use products that contain nicotine and tobacco. What actions can I take to prevent this?

In the hospital

A VTE may be prevented by taking medicines that are prescribed to prevent blood clots (anticoagulants). You can also help to prevent VTE while in the hospital by taking these actions:

¢ Get out of bed and walk. Ask your health care provider if this is safe for you to do. ¢ Ask your health care provider if you should use a sequential compression device (SCD). This is a machine that pumps air into compression sleeves that are wrapped around your legs.

¢ Ask your health care provider if you should wear tight, elastic stockings that apply pressure to the lower legs (compression stockings). Compression stockings are sometimes used with SCDs.

At home after surgery Understand that you have an increased risk for VTE for the first 4-6 weeks after surgery. During this time:

¢ Avoid traveling for more than 4 hours. If you must travel after surgery, ask your health care provider about additional preventive actions that you can take.

¢ Avoid sitting or lying still for too long. If possible, get up and walk around one time every hour. Ask your health care provider when this is safe for you to do.

While traveling

ETAT ATCT TTT Pale yellow

Travel that takes more than 4 hours can increase the risk of a VTE. To prevent VTE when traveling:

e Exercise your arms and legs every hour. You can do this by standing, stretching, and bending and straightening your arms and legs. If you are traveling by airplane, train, or bus, walk up and down the

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 45 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2277] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

aisle as often as possible to get your blood moving. If you are traveling by car, stop and get out of the car every hour to exercise your arms and legs and stretch. Other types of exercise might include: —46 Keeping your feet flat on the ground and raising your toes. —46 Switching from tightening the muscles in your calves and thighs to relaxing those same muscles while you are sitting. —46 Pointing and flexing your feet at the ankle joints while you are sitting. ¢ Drink enough water to keep your urine pale yellow.

¢ Avoid drinking alcohol during long travel.

Generally, it is not recommended that you take medicines to prevent DVT during routine travel.

Activity

¢ Stay active. Avoid sitting or lying in bed for long periods of time without moving your arms and legs. ¢ Exercise by moving your arms and legs for 30 minutes or more every day. ¢ Try not to bump or injure your legs.

¢ Avoid crossing your legs when you are sitting.

Lifestyle

¢ Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and chewing tobacco. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider. This is especially important if you take estrogen medicines.

¢ Avoid wearing tight clothing around your legs or waist.

General information

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 46 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-667] Account #: T[PHONE-668] Emergency by ro

¢ Wear compression stockings as told by your health care provider. These stockings help to prevent blood clots and reduce swelling in your legs. Do not let them bunch up when you are wearing them.

¢ Avoid using medicines that contain estrogen if you do not need them. These include birth control pills and hormone replacement therapy.

¢ Do not use pillows under your knees while lying down unless told by your health care provider.

¢ Maintain a healthy weight. Where to find more information

¢ Centers for Disease Control and Prevention: www.cdc.gov ¢ American Heart Association: www.heart.org

Get help right away if:

¢ You have chest pain or shortness of breath.

¢ You cough up blood.

¢ You have a rapid or irregular heartbeat.

¢ You feel light-headed or dizzy.

¢ You have new or increased pain, swelling, or redness in an arm or leg. ¢ You have numbness or tingling in an arm or leg.

¢ You have blood in your vomit, stool, or urine.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary

How to Use a Walker

This sheet gives you information about how to use a walker. Your health care provider may also:

¢ Give you more specific instructions based on your condition.

¢ Give you instructions on how to use your legs or arms to support your body weight (weight bearing).

What are the risks? Using a walker is generally safe. However, it may cause falls if it is not used correctly. How to walk with a walker

The best way to walk with a walker depends on whether you are using a standard walker or a front-wheeled walker. A standard walker has rubber tips on the ends of all four legs. A front-wheeled walker has wheels on the ends of the front legs and rubber tips on the ends of the back legs.

* Do not use your walker on stairs or an escalator unless you have been trained by a physical therapist and your health care provider approves.

Standard walker

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 48 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2271] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency =, ~

5.

Pick up your walker. Do not slide your standard walker.

Set down your walker, one step-length in front of you. Make sure that all four legs of the walker touch the ground at the same time. Your toes should be farther forward than the back legs of your walker. Hold on to the walker, and press it with your arms for support.

Step your weaker leg into the middle of the walker. Step your stronger leg forward to land next to your weaker leg.

Repeat this process for each step.

Front-wheeled walker

Tips

|

Slide your front-wheeled walker one step-length in front of you. Your toes should be farther forward

than the back legs of your walker. Hold on to the walker for support and press through the handrails as needed for support.

Step your weaker leg into the middle of the walker. Step your stronger leg forward to land next to your weaker leg. Repeat the process for each step.

Always keep both feet within the width of the walker's legs or wheels. When using your walker, you should not feel like you need to lean forward or to the side to keep your

hands on the handgrips.

¢ Make sure you are following any weight-bearing instructions that your health care provider gave you. ¢ Ifyou have a standard walker: —46 Do not slide your walker when you are moving. ¢ Ifyou have a front-wheeled walker: —46 Becareful not to let the walker get too far ahead of you as you walk. —46_ If your walker does not glide well over carpet, consider cutting an '"X" into two tennis balls and placing the balls over the back legs of your walker.

How to stand up with a walker

1. Put your walker in front of you.

2. Slide forward in your chair.

3. Position your legs so that your weaker leg is ahead of you and your stronger leg is bent and near your chair.

4. Position your hands. ° If your chair has armrests, put each hand on an armrest. ° If there are no armrests, put the hand opposite your weaker leg on the chair seat, and put the

other hand on the center of the walker's crossbar. . Lean forward and push up from your chair.

5 6. Rise by straightening your stronger leg. 7. Steady yourself.

8

Carefully move your hands to the handgrips of the walker. Tips

¢ Do not pull on the walker when you stand up. This may cause it to tip.

¢ Sit in a firm chair whenever you can. A low seat or an overstuffed chair or sofa is hard to get out of.

How to sit down with a walker Seat with armrests

1. Back up toward your seat, using your walker, until you feel the back of your legs touch the chair. 2. Carefully reach your hands behind you and put each hand on an armrest.

3. Slowly lower yourself into the seat with your injured leg out in front.

Seat without armrests

1. Back up toward the side of the seat, using your walker, until you feel the back of your legs touch the chair.

2. Use one hand to hold on to the back of the chair, and use the other hand to hold on to the front of the seat.

3. Slowly lower yourself into the seat.

How to use a walker on a curb or step Stepping up

1. Put all four legs of the walker on the curb or step. 2. Get your feet as close to the curb or step as you can. 3. Test the steadiness of the walker by pressing down on the handgrips. 4. Ifthe walker is steady, press down on it with your hands as you step up with your stronger leg. 5. Step up with your weaker leg. Stepping down

Put all four legs of the walker on the surface that is lower than the curb or step. Get your feet as close to the curb or step as you can.

Subcutaneous Injection Instructions Using a Prefilled Syringe

A subcutaneous injection is a shot of medicine that is given into the layer of fat and tissue between skin and muscle. The injection is given with a single-use syringe that is already filled with medicine (prefilled syringe).

Read the medicine guide or package insert that came with the syringe. Follow directions from the guide about how to prepare and give the injection. This is important because the directions may be different for each medicine. Use only the syringe, needle, and medicine that your health care provider prescribes. Use each prefilled syringe and needle only one time.

Supplies needed:

¢ Prefilled syringe with needle. Use the needle length and size (gauge) that your health care provider or pharmacist gives to you.

¢ Alcohol wipes.

¢ Bandage.

¢ Acontainer for syringe disposal. This may be a puncture-proof sharps container or a hard-sided plastic container that has a secure lid, such as an empty laundry detergent bottle.

How to choose a site for injection

) He

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 52 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2265] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

Follow instructions from your health care provider about where to give an injection. Do not inject in the same spot each time. There are five main areas that can be used for injecting. These areas include:

Abdomen. Avoid the area that is within 2 inches (5 cm) of your navel (umbilicus). Front of thigh.

Upper, outer side of thigh.

Upper, outer side of arm.

Upper, outer part of buttock.

How to give an injection using a prefilled syringe

PeYN

Wash your hands with soap and water. If soap and water are not available, use hand sanitizer.

Use an alcohol wipe to clean the site where you will be injecting the needle. Let the site air-dry. Remove the plastic cover from the needle on the syringe. Do not let the needle touch anything.

Hold the syringe with the needle pointing up. Check the syringe for any remaining air bubbles. If there are air bubbles, flick the syringe with your finger until the air bubbles rise to the top. Then, gently push

on the plunger until you can see a drop of medicine appear at the tip of the needle. This will clear any remaining air bubbles from the syringe.

5. Hold the syringe in your writing hand like a pencil.

Use your other hand to pinch and hold about an inch (2.5 cm) of skin. Do not directly touch the cleaned part of the skin.

Insert the entire needle straight into the fold of skin. The needle should be at a 90-degree angle (perpendicular) to the skin. Push the needle all the way against the skin. The needle may need to be injected at a 45-degree angle in thin adults or children who have a small amount of body fat.

After the needle is completely inserted into the skin, release the skin that you are pinching. Continue to hold the syringe with your writing hand.

Use your thumb or index finger of your writing hand to push the plunger all the way into the syringe to

inject the medicine.

10. Pull the needle straight out of the skin.

11. Press and hold the alcohol wipe over the injection site until bleeding stops. Do not rub the area.

12. Cover the injection site with a bandage, if needed.

How to safely throw away the supplies

If you are using a syringe that does not have a safety system for shielding the needle after injection: ¢ Do not recap the needle. Place the syringe and needle in the disposal container.

If your syringe has a safety system for shielding the needle after injection:

¢ Firmly push down on the plunger after you complete the injection. The protective sleeve will

automatically cover the needle, and you will hear a click. The click means that the needle 1s safely covered.

Follow the disposal regulations for the area where you live. Do not use any syringe or needle more than one time.

Contact a health care provider if:

¢ You have difficulty giving the injection.

¢ You think that the injection was not given correctly. ¢ You have difficulty with any of the supplies.

¢ The medicine causes side effects.

¢ Rashes develop on the skin.

e A fever develops.

¢ The condition that is being treated gets worse.

Get help right away if: Any of these symptoms develop after the injection is given:

¢ Difficulty breathing.

¢ Chest pain.

¢ Arash over most or all of the body. ¢ Swelling of the lips or tongue.

¢ Difficulty swallowing.

Opioid Pain Medicine Management

Opioid pain medicines are strong medicines that are used to treat bad or very bad pain. When you take them for a short time, they can help you:

¢ Sleep better.

¢ Do better in physical therapy.

¢ Feel better during the first few days after you get hurt.

¢ Recover from surgery.

Only take these medicines if a doctor says that you can. You should only take them for a short time. This is because opioids can be hard to stop taking (they are addictive). The longer you take opioids, the harder it may be to stop taking them (opioid use disorder).

What are the risks?

Opioids can cause problems (side effects). Taking them for more than 3 days raises your chance of problems, such as:

¢ Trouble pooping (constipation).

¢ Feeling sick to your stomach (nausea).

¢ Vomiting.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 55 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient. DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-683] Account #: T[PHONE-684] Emergency ¢ Feeling very sleepy. ¢ Confusion.

Not being able to stop taking the medicine. Breathing problems.

Taking opioids for a long time can make it hard for you to do daily tasks. It can also put you at risk for:

Car accidents. Depression. Suicide. Heart attack.

Taking too much of the medicine (overdose), which can sometimes lead to death.

What is a pain treatment plan?

A pain treatment plan is a plan made by you and your doctor. Work with your doctor to make a plan for treating your pain. To help you do this:

Talk about the goals of your treatment, including:

—46 How much pain you might expect to have.

—46 How you will manage the pain.

Talk about the risks and benefits of taking these medicines for your condition.

Remember that a good treatment plan uses more than one approach and lowers the risks of side effects. Tell your doctor about the amount of medicines you take and about any drug or alcohol use.

Get your pain medicine prescriptions from only one doctor.

Pain can be managed with other treatments. Work with your doctor to find other ways to help your pain, such

as:

Physical therapy. Counseling.

Eating healthy foods. Brain exercises. Massage. Meditation.

¢ Other pain medicines.

* Doing gentle exercises.

Tapering your use of opioids

If you have been taking opioids for more than a few weeks, you may need to slowly decrease (taper) how much you take until you stop taking them. Doing this can lower your chance of having symptoms, such as:

¢ Pain and cramping in your belly (abdomen).

¢ Feeling sick to your stomach.

¢ Sweating.

¢ Feeling very sleepy.

¢ Feeling restless.

¢ Shaking you cannot control (tremors).

¢ Cravings for the medicine.

Do not try to stop taking them by yourself. Work with your doctor to stop. Your doctor will help you take less until you are not taking the medicine at all.

Follow these instructions at home:

Safety and storage

¢ While you are taking opioids: —46 Do not drive. —46 Do not use machines or power tools. —46 Do not sign important papers (legal documents). —46 Do not drink alcohol. —46 Do not take sleeping pills. —46 Do not take care of children by yourself.

—46 Do not do activities where you need to climb or be in high places, like working on a ladder.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 57 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2257] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

—46 Do not go into any water, such as a lake, river, ocean, swimming pool, or hot tub. ¢ Keep your opioids locked up or in a place where children cannot reach them.

* Do not share your pain medicine with anyone.

Getting rid of leftover pills Do not save any leftover pills. Get rid of leftover pills safely by:

¢ Taking them to a take-back program in your area. ¢ Bringing them to a pharmacy that has a container for throwing away pills (pill disposal). ¢ Throwing them in the trash. Check the label or package insert of your medicine to see whether this is safe to do. If it is safe to throw them out: 1. Take the pills out of their container. 2. Mix the pills with pet poop or food scraps. 3. Put this in the trash.

Activity

¢ Return to your normal activities as told by your doctor. Ask your doctor what activities are safe for you. ¢ Avoid doing things that make your pain worse.

* Do exercises as told by your doctor. General instructions

¢ You may need to take these actions to prevent or treat trouble pooping: —46 Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) pale yellow. —46 Take over-the-counter or prescription medicines. —46 Eat foods that are high in fiber. These include beans, whole grains, and fresh fruits and

vegetables. —46 Limit foods that are high in fat and sugar. These include fried or sweet foods.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Where to find support

If you have been taking opioids for a long time, think about getting help quitting from a local support group or counselor. Ask your doctor about this.

Where to find more information

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC): www.cdc.gov Get help right away if:

Seek medical care right away if you are taking opioids and you, or people close to you, notice any of the following:

¢ You have trouble breathing.

¢ Your breathing is slower or more shallow than normal.

¢ You have a very slow heartbeat.

¢ You feel very confused.

¢ You pass out (faint).

¢ You are very sleepy.

¢ Your speech is not normal.

¢ You feel sick to your stomach and vomit.

¢ You have cold skin.

* You have blue lips or fingernails.

¢ Your muscles are weak (limp) and your body seems floppy.

¢ The black centers of your eyes (pupils) are smaller than normal.

If you think that you or someone else may have taken too much of an opioid medicine, get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

If you ever feel like you may hurt yourself or others, or have thoughts about taking your own life, get help right away. You can go to your nearest emergency department or call:

¢ Your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.).

¢ The hotline of the National Poison Control Center (1-[PHONE-2255] in the U.S.).

¢ A suicide crisis helpline, such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-[PHONE-689]. This is open 24 hours a day.

Summary

¢ Opioid are strong medicines that are used to treat bad or very bad pain.

¢ A pain treatment plan is a plan made by you and your doctor. Work with your doctor to make a plan for treating your pain.

¢ Work with your doctor to find other ways to help your pain.

Opioid Overdose

Opioids are drugs that are often used to treat pain. Opioids include illegal drugs, such as heroin, as well as prescription pain medicines, such as codeine, morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone, and fentanyl. An opioid overdose happens when you take too much of an opioid. An overdose may be intentional or accidental and can happen with any type of opioid.

The effects of an overdose can be mild, dangerous, or even deadly. Opioid overdose is a medical emergency. What are the causes? This condition may be caused by:

¢ Taking too much of an opioid on purpose. ¢ Taking too much of an opioid by accident. ¢ Using two or more substances that contain opioids at the same time.

¢ Taking an opioid with a substance that affects your heart, breathing, or blood pressure. These include alcohol, tranquilizers, sleeping pills, illegal drugs, and some over-the-counter medicines.

This condition may also happen due to an error made by:

¢ A health care provider who prescribes a medicine.

¢ The pharmacist who fills the prescription order.

What increases the risk?

This condition is more likely in:

¢ Children. They may be attracted to colorful pills. Because of a child's small size, even a small amount of a drug can be dangerous.

¢ Older people. They may be taking many different drugs. Older people may have difficulty reading labels or remembering when they last took their medicine. They may also be more sensitive to the effects of opioids.

¢ People with chronic medical conditions, especially heart, liver, kidney, or neurological diseases. ¢ People who take an opioid for a long period of time. ¢ People who use: —46 Illegal drugs. IV heroin is especially dangerous. —46 Other substances, including alcohol, while using an opioid. ¢ People who have: —46 A history of drug or alcohol abuse. —46 Certain mental health conditions. —46 A history of previous drug overdoses.

¢ People who take opioids that are not prescribed for them.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Symptoms of this condition depend on the type of opioid and the amount that was taken. Common symptoms include:

¢ Sleepiness or difficulty waking from sleep.

¢ Decrease in attention.

¢ Confusion.

¢ Slurred speech.

¢ Slowed breathing and a slow pulse (bradycardia).

¢ Nausea and vomiting.

e Abnormally small pupils. Signs and symptoms that require emergency treatment include:

¢ Cold, clammy, and pale skin.

¢ Blue lips and fingernails.

¢ Vomiting.

¢ Gurgling sounds in the throat.

¢ A pulse that is very slow or difficult to detect.

¢ Breathing that is very irregular, slow, noisy, or difficult to detect.

¢ Limp body.

¢ Inability to respond to speech or be awakened from sleep (stupor).

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 61 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2249] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ Seizures.

How is this diagnosed?

This condition is diagnosed based on your symptoms and medical history. It is important to tell your health care provider:

¢ About all of the opioids that you took. ¢ When you took the opioids.

¢ Whether you were drinking alcohol or using marijuana, cocaine, or other drugs. Your health care provider will do a physical exam. This exam may include:

¢ Checking and monitoring your heart rate and rhythm, breathing rate, temperature, and blood pressure (vital signs). ¢ Measuring oxygen levels in your blood.

¢ Checking for abnormally small pupils. You may also have blood tests or urine tests. You may have X-rays if you are having severe breathing problems. How is this treated?

This condition requires immediate medical treatment and hospitalization. Treatment is given in the hospital intensive care (ICU) setting. Supporting your vital signs and your breathing is the first step in treating an opioid overdose. Treatment may also include:

¢ Giving salts and minerals (electrolytes) along with fluids through an IV.

¢ Inserting a breathing tube (endotracheal tube) in your airway to help you breathe if you cannot breathe on your own or you are in danger of not being able to breathe on your own.

¢ Giving oxygen through a small tube under your nose.

¢ Passing a tube through your nose and into your stomach (nasogastric tube, or NG tube) to empty your stomach.

¢ Giving medicines that: —46 Increase your blood pressure. —46_ Relieve nausea and vomiting. —46 Relieve abdominal pain and cramping. —46 Reverse the effects of the opioid (naloxone).

¢ Monitoring your heart and oxygen levels.

¢ Ongoing counseling and mental health support if you intentionally overdosed or used an illegal drug.

Follow these instructions at home:

Fan MA NT me ITA) Pale yellow Medicines

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

e Always ask your health care provider about possible side effects and interactions of any new medicine that you start taking.

¢ Keep a list of all the medicines that you take, including over-the-counter medicines. Bring this list with you to all your medical visits.

General instructions

¢ Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

How is this prevented?

¢ Read the drug inserts that come with your opioid pain medicines. ¢ Take medicines only as told by your health care provider. Do not take more medicine than you are told. Do not take medicines more frequently than you are told. ¢ Do not drink alcohol or take sedatives when taking opioids. ¢ Do not use illegal or recreational drugs, including cocaine, ecstasy, and marijuana. ¢ Do not take opioid medicines that are not prescribed for you. ¢ Store all medicines in safety containers that are out of the reach of children. ¢ Get help if you are struggling with: —46 Alcohol or drug use. —46 Depression or another mental health problem. —46 Thoughts of hurting yourself or another person.

* Keep the phone number of your local poison control center near your phone or in your mobile phone. In the U.S., the hotline of the National Poison Control Center is [PHONE-696].

¢ Ifyou were prescribed naloxone, make sure you understand how to take it.

Contact a health care provider if you:

* Need help understanding how to take your pain medicines. ¢ Feel your medicines are too strong. e Are concerned that your pain medicines are not working well for your pain.

¢ Develop new symptoms or side effects when you are taking medicines.

Get help right away if:

¢ You or someone else is having symptoms of an opioid overdose. Get help even if you are not sure. ¢ You have serious thoughts about hurting yourself or others. ¢ You have:

—46 Chest pain.

—46 Difficulty breathing.

—46 A loss of consciousness.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

If you ever feel like you may hurt yourself or others, or have thoughts about taking your own life, get help right away. You can go to your nearest emergency department or call:

¢ Your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.).

¢ A suicide crisis helpline, such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-[PHONE-2245]. This is open 24 hours a day.

Summary

¢ Opioids are drugs that are often used to treat pain. Opioids include illegal drugs, such as heroin, as well as prescription pain medicines.

¢ An opioid overdose happens when you take too much of an opioid.

¢ Overdoses can be intentional or accidental.

¢ Opioid overdose is very dangerous. It is a life-threatening emergency.

¢ If you or someone you know is experiencing an opioid overdose, get help right away.

Musculoskeletal Pain

Musculoskeletal pain refers to aches and pains in your bones, joints, muscles, and the tissues that surround them. This pain can occur in any part of the body. It can last for a short time (acute) or a long time (chronic).

A physical exam, lab tests, and imaging studies may be done to find the cause of your musculoskeletal pain.

Follow these instructions at home:

Lifestyle

¢ Try to control or lower your stress levels. Stress increases muscle tension and can worsen

musculoskeletal pain. It is important to recognize when you are anxious or stressed and learn ways to manage it. This may include: —46 Meditation or yoga.

—46 Cognitive or behavioral therapy. —46 Acupuncture or massage therapy.

¢ You may continue all activities unless the activities cause more pain. When the pain gets better, slowly resume your normal activities. Gradually increase the intensity and duration of your activities or exercise.

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 65 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2241] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ When your pain is severe, bed rest may be helpful. Lie or sit in any position that is comfortable, but get out of bed and walk around at least every couple of hours. ¢ Ifdirected, apply heat to the affected area as often as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source that your health care provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. —46_ Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. —46 Remove the heat if your skin turns bright red. This is especially important if you are unable to feel pain, heat, or cold. You may have a greater risk of getting burned. ¢ If directed, put ice on the painful area. —46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag.

—46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. General instructions

¢ Your health care provider may recommend that you see a physical therapist. This person can help you come up with a safe exercise program. Do any exercises as told by your physical therapist.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits, including any physical therapy visits, as told by your health care providers. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if:

¢ Your pain gets worse.

¢ Medicines do not help ease your pain.

¢ You cannot use the part of your body that hurts, such as your arm, leg, or neck. ¢ You have trouble sleeping.

¢ You have trouble doing your normal activities.

Get help right away if:

¢ You have a new injury and your pain is worse or different.

¢ You feel numb or you have tingling in the painful area.

Summary

¢ Musculoskeletal pain refers to aches and pains in your bones, joints, muscles, and the tissues that surround them.

¢ This pain can occur in any part of the body.

¢ Your health care provider may recommend that you see a physical therapist. This person can help you come up with a safe exercise program. Do any exercises as told by your physical therapist.

¢ Lower your stress level. Stress can worsen musculoskeletal pain. Ways to lower stress may include meditation, yoga, cognitive or behavioral therapy, acupuncture, and massage therapy.

How to Use Cold Therapy

Cold therapy, or cryotherapy, is a treatment that uses cold temperatures to treat an injury or medical condition. It includes using cold packs or ice packs to reduce pain and swelling. Only use cold therapy if your doctor says it is okay.

What are the risks? Generally, cold therapy is a safe treatment. However, it is not safe for:

¢ People who are not able to say they are in pain. These include small children and people who have memory problems.

¢ People who have certain conditions, such as: —46 A problem in the vessels that slows blood flow to the fingers and toes (Raynaud's syndrome). —46 Feeling very cold easily (cold hypersensitivity). —46 Lack of feeling in the area being iced.

Cold therapy may not be safe for people who have other conditions. Do not use it without talking to your doctor if you have:

¢ <A heart condition.

¢ High blood pressure.

¢ Open or healing wounds.

¢ An infection.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 67 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2237] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

¢ Pain and swelling in your joints (rheumatoid arthritis). ¢ Poor blood flow in the body. ¢ Diabetes.

¢ Certain skin conditions.

How can I make a cold pack? When using a cold pack at home to reduce pain and swelling, you can use:

¢ A silica gel cold pack that has been left in the freezer. You can buy this online or in stores. ¢ A sealable plastic bag that has been filled with crushed ice. ¢ A washcloth or paper towels soaked in cold (or ice) water.

¢ Aplastic bag of frozen vegetables. Throw them away when you are finished using them as a cold pack.

Supplies needed:

¢ Acold pack. ¢ A towel. This can be dry or damp, based on what you like.

How to use cold therapy

Have your cold pack ready. Place a towel between the cold pack and your skin. You may also wrap the cold pack in a towel.

Put the cold pack on the affected area. Keep it on for no more than 20 minutes at a time.

PeYN

Check your skin after 5 minutes to make sure that there is no damage to the area. Check for: ° White spots on your skin. Your skin may look blotchy or mottled.

° Skin that looks blue or pale.

° Skin that feels waxy or hard.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 68 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2236] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

5. Repeat these steps as many times each day as told by your doctor. Always use a towel to avoid direct contact with your skin. Contact a doctor if:

¢ You start to have white spots on your skin. This may give your skin a blotchy or mottled look. ¢ Your skin turns blue or pale. ¢ Your skin becomes waxy or hard.

¢ Your swelling gets worse.

Summary

¢ Cold therapy, or cryotherapy, is used to treat an injury or other conditions. It includes using cold packs or ice packs to reduce pain and swelling.

¢ Cold therapy is not safe for people who are not able to say they are in pain.

¢ When using cold packs or ice packs, always place a towel between the cold source and your skin.

¢ Check your skin after 5 minutes of icing it. This is to make sure that there is no skin damage.

¢ Contact your doctor if you notice changes in your skin or your swelling gets worse.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Constipation, Adult

Constipation is when a person:

¢ Poops (has a bowel movement) fewer times in a week than normal. ¢ Has a hard time pooping.

¢ Has poop that is dry, hard, or bigger than normal.

Follow these instructions at home:

Eating and drinking

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 69 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

¢ Eat foods that have a lot of fiber, such as: —46 Fresh fruits and vegetables. —46 Whole grains. —46 Beans. ¢ Eat less of foods that are high in fat, low in fiber, or overly processed, such as: —46 French fries. —46 Hamburgers. —46 Cookies. —46 Candy. —46 Soda.

¢ Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) clear or pale yellow. General instructions

e Exercise regularly or as told by your doctor.

* Go to the restroom when you feel like you need to poop. Do not hold it in.

¢ Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor. These include any fiber supplements.

* Do pelvic floor retraining exercises, such as: —46 Doing deep breathing while relaxing your lower belly (abdomen). —46_ Relaxing your pelvic floor while pooping.

¢ Watch your condition for any changes.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important. Contact a doctor if:

¢ You have pain that gets worse.

Orthopedics

Walking Boot, Adult

A walking boot is a medical device that holds your foot or ankle in place after an injury or a medical procedure. This helps with healing and prevents further injury. A walking boot is a removable boot-shaped splint. It has a hard, rigid outer frame that limits movement and supports your foot and leg. The inner lining is a layer of padded material. Walking boots usually have adjustable straps to secure them over the foot and leg.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 71 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2229] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

Your health care provider may prescribe a walking boot if you can put weight (bear weight) on your injured foot. How much you can walk while wearing the boot will depend on the type and severity of your injury. Your health care provider will recommend the best boot for you based on your condition.

How do I put on my walking boot?

There are different types of walking boots. Each type of boot has specific instructions about how to wear it properly. Follow instructions from your health care provider about wearing your boot. In general:

e Ask someone to help you put on the boot, if needed.

¢ Sit to put on your boot. Doing this is more comfortable and it helps to prevent falls.

¢ Open up the boot fully. Place your foot into the boot so your heel rests against the back.

¢ Your toes should be supported by the base of the boot. They should not hang over the front edge.

¢ Adjust the straps so the boot fits securely but is not too tight.

¢ Do not bend the hard frame of the boot to get a good fit.

What are some tips for walking with a walking boot?

¢ Do not try to walk without wearing the boot unless your health care provider has approved. ¢ Use other assistive walking devices, including crutches and canes, as told by your health care provider. ¢ On your uninjured foot, wear a shoe with a heel that is close to the height of the walking boot.

¢ Be careful when walking on surfaces that are uneven or wet.

How can I reduce swelling while using a walking boot?

¢ Rest your injured foot or leg as much as possible.

¢ Ifdirected, apply ice to the injured area:

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 72 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2228] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

—46 Put ice ina plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. ¢ Keep your injured foot or leg raised (elevated) above the level of your heart whenever able. Try to do this for at least 2@2Bours each day or as told by your health care provider. ¢ Ifswelling gets worse, loosen the boot and rest and elevate your foot and leg.

How should I take care of my skin and foot while using a walking boot?

¢ Wear a long sock to protect your foot and leg from rubbing inside the boot. ¢ Take off the boot one time each day to check the injured area. Look at your foot, surrounding skin, and

leg to make sure there are no sores, rashes, swelling, or wounds. The skin should be a healthy color, not pale or blue.

¢ Try to notice if your walking pattern (gait) in the boot is fairly normal and that you are not walking with a noticeable limp.

¢ Follow instructions from your health care provider about taking care of your incision or wound, if this applies.

¢ Clean and wash the injured area as told by your health care provider.

¢ Gently dry your foot and leg before putting the boot back on.

Are there any activity restrictions?

Activity restrictions depend on the type and severity of your injury. Follow instructions from your health care provider about limiting activities.

¢ Bathe and shower as told by your health care provider. ¢ Do not do any activities that could make your injury worse.

* Do not drive if your affected foot is one that you usually use for driving.

When can I remove my walking boot?

Always follow specific directions from your health care provider for removing the walking boot. Generally, it is okay to remove your walking boot:

¢ At the end of the day when you are resting or sleeping.

¢ To clean your foot and leg.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 73 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2227] Account #: 72110750002 Emergency

How should I keep my walking boot clean?

¢ Do not put any part of the boot in a washing machine or dryer.

¢ Do not use chemical cleaning products. These could irritate your skin, especially if you have a wound or an incision.

* Do not soak the liner of the boot.

¢ Use a washcloth with mild soap and water to clean the frame and the liner of the boot by hand.

¢ Allow the boot to air-dry completely before you put it back on your foot.

Contact a health care provider if:

¢ The boot is cracked or damaged.

¢ The boot does not fit properly.

¢ Your foot or leg hurts.

¢ You have a rash, sore, or open sore (ulcer) on your foot or leg.

¢ The skin on your foot or leg is pale.

¢ You have a wound or incision on the foot and it is getting worse. ¢ Your skin becomes painful, red, or irritated.

¢ Your swelling does not get better or it gets worse.

Get help right away if:

¢ You cannot feel your foot or leg (have numbness). ¢ You cannot feel a pulse at the top of your foot, where your foot and ankle meet.

¢ Your skin on the foot or leg is cold, blue, or gray.

Summary

¢ A walking boot holds your foot or ankle in place after an injury or a medical procedure.

¢ There are different types of walking boots. Follow the specific instructions about how to correctly wear the boot that you have.

e Ask someone to help you put on the boot, if needed.

¢ It is important to check your skin and foot every day. Call your health care provider if you notice a rash or sore on your foot or leg.
