° You have: —46 Loss of feeling (numbness), tingling, or weakness in your arms or legs. —46 Very bad neck pain, especially tenderness in the middle of the back of your neck. —46 A change in your ability to control your pee or poop (stool). —46 More pain in any area of your body. —46 Swelling in any area of your body, especially your legs. —46 Shortness of breath or light-headedness. —46 Chest pain. —46 Blood in your pee, poop, or vomit. —46 Very bad pain in your belly (abdomen) or your back. —46 Very bad headaches or headaches that are getting worse. —46 Sudden vision loss or double vision. ° Your eye suddenly turns red. ° The black center of your eye (pupil) is an odd shape or size.

Summary

° After a car accident (motor vehicle collision), it is common to have injuries to your head, face, arms, and body. ° Follow instructions from your doctor about how to take care of a wound or burn.

° If told, put ice on your injured areas.

° Contact a doctor if your symptoms get worse.

° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor.

Incision Care

An incision (cut) is when a surgeon cuts into your body. After surgery, the cut needs to be well cared for to keep it from getting infected.

°OSEIF de ASSOCIATES. INC, B¥S

Discharge Planning

HOW TO CARE FOR YOUR CUT * Take medicines only as told by your doctor. ¢ There are many different ways to close and cover a cut, including stitches, skin glue, and adhesive strips. Follow your doctor's

instructions on: Remove the right leg post-operative dressing on Friday, 4/23/21. Then, begin to clean the incisions with antibacterial soap and water and rinse and pat dry. If needed due to drainage or skin irritation or CAM boot wear then cover with a dry dressings changing daily and as needed.

—Qare of the cut.

—Biandage (dressing) changes and removal.

—Qiat closure removal.

* Do not take baths, swim, or use a hot tub until your doctor says it is okay. You may shower as told by your doctor. ¢ Return to your normal diet and activities as allowed by your doctor.

¢ Use medicine that helps lessen itching on your cut as told by your doctor. Do not pick or scratch at your cut.

* Drink enough fluids to keep your pee (urine) clear or pale yellow.

GET HELP IF:

* You have redness, puffiness (swelling), or pain at the site of your cut.

* You have fluid, blood, or pus coming from your cut.

¢ Your muscles ache.

* You have chills or you feel sick.

* You have a bad smell coming from the cut or bandage.

* Your cut opens up after stitches, staples, or adhesive strips have been removed. * You keep feeling sick to your stomach (nauseous) or keep throwing up (vomiting). * You have a fever.

* You are dizzy.

GET HELP RIGHT AWAY IF: * You have a rash.

* You pass out (faint).

* You have trouble breathing.

MAKE SURE YOU:

¢ Understand these instructions.

¢ Will watch your condition.

* Will get help right away if you are not doing well or get worse.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Venous Thromboembolism Prevention

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) is a condition in which a blood clot (thrombus) develops in the body. A deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot that usually occurs in a deep vein in the leg or the pelvis, but it can also occur in the arm.

Sometimes, pieces of a thrombus can break off and travel through the bloodstream to other parts of the body. When that happens, the

thrombus is called an embolus. An embolus that travels to the lungs is called a pulmonary embolism. An embolism can block the blood flow in the blood vessels of other organs as well.

How can this condition affect me?

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 790 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2189] Account #: T[PHONE-2190]

Discharge Planning

VTE is a serious health condition that can cause disability or death. It is very important to get help right away. Do not ignore your symptoms.

What can increase my risk? You are more likely to develop this condition if you:

° Have had recent major surgery or trauma.

. Have certain health conditions, such as cancer.

¢ Are in the hospital for a sudden illness.

° Have not moved for a long period of time, such as if you are on bed rest or traveling a long distance. . Take certain medicines, such as birth control pills or hormone replacement therapy.

° Are pregnant or recently gave birth.

. Have a personal or family history of VTE.

° Are overweight.

° Are 60 years of age or older.

° Use products that contain nicotine and tobacco.

What actions can | take to prevent this?

In the hospital

A VTE may be prevented by taking medicines that are prescribed to prevent blood clots (anticoagulants). You can also help to prevent VTE while in the hospital by taking these actions:

° Get out of bed and walk. Ask your health care provider if this is safe for you to do.

° Ask your health care provider if you should use a sequential compression device (SCD). This is a machine that pumps air into compression sleeves that are wrapped around your legs.

° Ask your health care provider if you should wear tight, elastic stockings that apply pressure to the lower legs (compression stockings). Compression stockings are sometimes used with SCDs.

At home after surgery Understand that you have an increased risk for VTE for the first 4-6 weeks after surgery. During this time:

° Avoid traveling for more than 4 hours. If you must travel after surgery, ask your health care provider about additional preventive actions that you can take.

. Avoid sitting or lying still for too long. If possible, get up and walk around one time every hour. Ask your health care provider when this is safe for you to do.

While traveling

ETAT ATCT TTT Pale yellow

Discharge Planning

Travel that takes more than 4 hours can increase the risk of a VTE. To prevent VTE when traveling:

° Exercise your arms and legs every hour. You can do this by standing, stretching, and bending and straightening your arms and legs. If you are traveling by airplane, train, or bus, walk up and down the aisle as often as possible to get your blood moving. If you are traveling by car, stop and get out of the car every hour to exercise your arms and legs and stretch. Other types of exercise might include: —46 Keeping your feet flat on the ground and raising your toes. —46 Switching from tightening the muscles in your calves and thighs to relaxing those same muscles while you are sitting. —46 Pointing and flexing your feet at the ankle joints while you are sitting.

° Drink enough water to keep your urine pale yellow.

° Avoid drinking alcohol during long travel.

Generally, it is not recommended that you take medicines to prevent DVT during routine travel.

Activity

° Stay active. Avoid sitting or lying in bed for long periods of time without moving your arms and legs. ° Exercise by moving your arms and legs for 30 minutes or more every day.

° Try not to bump or injure your legs.

° Avoid crossing your legs when you are sitting.

Lifestyle

° Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and chewing tobacco. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider. This is especially important if you take estrogen medicines. ° Avoid wearing tight clothing around your legs or waist.

General information

5

on ww ° Wear compression stockings as told by your health care provider. These stockings help to prevent blood clots and reduce swelling in your legs. Do not let them bunch up when you are wearing them.

Discharge Planning

° Avoid using medicines that contain estrogen if you do not need them. These include birth control pills and hormone replacement therapy.

° Do not use pillows under your knees while lying down unless told by your health care provider.

. Maintain a healthy weight.

Where to find more information

° Centers for Disease Control and Prevention: www.cdc.gov ° American Heart Association: www.heart.org

Get help right away if:

° You have chest pain or shortness of breath.

° You cough up blood.

° You have a rapid or irregular heartbeat.

* You feel light-headed or dizzy.

. You have new or increased pain, swelling, or redness in an arm or leg. . You have numbness or tingling in an arm or leg.

° You have blood in your vomit, stool, or urine.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary

. Venous thromboembolism (VTE) is a condition in which a blood clot (thrombus)develops in the body. A deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot that usually occurs in a deep vein in the leg or the pelvis, but it can also occur in the arm.

° VTE is a serious health condition that can cause disability or death. It is very important to get help right away. Do not ignore your symptoms.

° If you are traveling, exercise your arms and legs every hour.

° Stay active to help prevent blood clots. Avoid sitting or lying in bed for long periods of time without moving your arms and legs.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

How to Use a Walker This sheet gives you information about how to use a walker. Your health care provider may also:

° Give you more specific instructions based on your condition. . Give you instructions on how to use your legs or arms to support your body weight (weight bearing).

What are the risks?

Using a walker is generally safe. However, it may cause falls if it is not used correctly.

How to walk with a walker

The best way to walk with a walker depends on whether you are using a standard walker or a front-wheeled walker. A standard walker has rubber tips on the ends of all four legs. A front-wheeled walker has wheels on the ends of the front legs and rubber tips on the ends of the back legs.

° Do not use your walker on stairs or an escalator unless you have been trained by a physical therapist and your health care provider approves.

Standard walker =, =

1. Pick up your walker. Do not slide your standard walker.

2. Set down your walker, one step-length in front of you. Make sure that all four legs of the walker touch the ground at the same time. Your toes should be farther forward than the back legs of your walker.

3. Hold on to the walker, and press it with your arms for support.

4. Step your weaker leg into the middle of the walker. Step your stronger leg forward to land next to your weaker leg.

5. Repeat this process for each step.

Front-wheeled walker

|

1. | Slide your front-wheeled walker one step-length in front of you. Your toes should be farther forward than the back legs of your walker.

2. Hold on to the walker for support and press through the handrails as needed for support. 3. Step your weaker leg into the middle of the walker. Step your stronger leg forward to land next to your weaker leg. 4. Repeat the process for each step.

Tips

. Always keep both feet within the width of the walker's legs or wheels. ° When using your walker, you should not feel like you need to lean forward or to the side to keep your hands on the handgrips.

—46 Do not slide your walker when you are moving. . If you have a front-wheeled walker:

—46 Be careful not to let the walker get too far ahead of you as you walk.

—46 If your walker does not glide well over carpet, consider cutting an "X" into two tennis balls and placing the balls over the back legs of your walker.

How to stand up with a walker

Put your walker in front of you.

Slide forward in your chair.

Position your legs so that your weaker leg is ahead of you and your stronger leg is bent and near your chair.

Position your hands.

° If your chair has armrests, put each hand on an armrest.

° If there are no armrests, put the hand opposite your weaker leg on the chair seat, and put the other hand on the center of the walker's crossbar.

Lean forward and push up from your chair.

Rise by straightening your stronger leg.

Steady yourself.

Carefully move your hands to the handgrips of the walker.

Pon Ss

Oo NON

Tips

. Do not pull on the walker when you stand up. This may cause it to tip. ° Sit in a firm chair whenever you can. A low seat or an overstuffed chair or sofa is hard to get out of.

How to sit down with a walker Seat with armrests

1. Back up toward your seat, using your walker, until you feel the back of your legs touch the chair. 2. Carefully reach your hands behind you and put each hand on an armrest. 3. Slowly lower yourself into the seat with your injured leg out in front.

Seat without armrests

1. Back up toward the side of the seat, using your walker, until you feel the back of your legs touch the chair. 2. Use one hand to hold on to the back of the chair, and use the other hand to hold on to the front of the seat. 3. Slowly lower yourself into the seat.

How to use a walker on a curb or step

Stepping up

Subcutaneous Injection Instructions Using a Prefilled Syringe

A subcutaneous injection is a shot of medicine that is given into the layer of fat and tissue between skin and muscle. The injection is given with a single-use syringe that is already filled with medicine (prefilled syringe).

Read the medicine guide or package insert that came with the syringe. Follow directions from the guide about how to prepare and give the injection. This is important because the directions may be different for each medicine. Use only the syringe, needle, and medicine that your health care provider prescribes. Use each prefilled syringe and needle only one time.

Supplies needed:

° Prefilled syringe with needle. Use the needle length and size (gauge) that your health care provider or pharmacist gives to you. ° Alcohol wipes.

How to choose a site for injection

Follow instructions from your health care provider about where to give an injection. Do not inject in the same spot each time. There are five main areas that can be used for injecting. These areas include:

Abdomen. Avoid the area that is within 2 inches (5 cm) of your navel (umbilicus). Front of thigh.

Upper, outer side of thigh.

Upper, outer side of arm.

Upper, outer part of buttock.

How to give an injection using a prefilled syringe

PowON >

a

| £4

Wash your hands with soap and water. If soap and water are not available, use hand sanitizer.

Use an alcohol wipe to clean the site where you will be injecting the needle. Let the site air-dry.

Remove the plastic cover from the needle on the syringe. Do not let the needle touch anything.

Hold the syringe with the needle pointing up. Check the syringe for any remaining air bubbles. If there are air bubbles, flick the syringe with your finger until the air bubbles rise to the top. Then, gently push on the plunger until you can see a drop of medicine appear at the tip of the needle. This will clear any remaining air bubbles from the syringe.

Hold the syringe in your writing hand like a pencil.

Use your other hand to pinch and hold about an inch (2.5 cm) of skin. Do not directly touch the cleaned part of the skin.

Discharge Planning

Insert the entire needle straight into the fold of skin. The needle should be at a 90-degree angle (perpendicular) to the skin.

Push the needle all the way against the skin. The needle may need to be injected at a 45-degree angle in thin adults or children who have a small amount of body fat.

After the needle is completely inserted into the skin, release the skin that you are pinching. Continue to hold the syringe with your writing hand. Use your thumb or index finger of your writing hand to push the plunger all the way into the syringe to inject the medicine.

10. Pull the needle straight out of the skin. 11. Press and hold the alcohol wipe over the injection site until bleeding stops. Do not rub the area. 12. Cover the injection site with a bandage, if needed.

How to safely throw away the supplies

If you are using a syringe that does not have a safety system for shielding the needle after injection:

Do not recap the needle. Place the syringe and needle in the disposal container.

If your syringe has a safety system for shielding the needle after injection:

Firmly push down on the plunger after you complete the injection. The protective sleeve will automatically cover the needle, and you will hear a click. The click means that the needle is safely covered.

Follow the disposal regulations for the area where you live. Do not use any syringe or needle more than one time.

Contact a health care provider if:

You have difficulty giving the injection.

You think that the injection was not given correctly. You have difficulty with any of the supplies.

The medicine causes side effects.

Rashes develop on the skin.

A fever develops.

The condition that is being treated gets worse.

Get help right away if:

Any of these symptoms develop after the injection is given:

Difficulty breathing.

Chest pain.

A rash over most or all of the body. Swelling of the lips or tongue. Difficulty swallowing.

Summary

A subcutaneous injection is a shot of medicine that is given into the layer of fat and tissue between skin and muscle.

Read the medicine guide or package insert that came with the syringe. Follow directions from the guide about how to prepare and give the injection.

Opioid Pain Medicine Management

Opioid pain medicines are strong medicines that are used to treat bad or very bad pain. When you take them for a short time, they can help you:

. Sleep better.

° Do better in physical therapy.

° Feel better during the first few days after you get hurt. ° Recover from surgery.

Only take these medicines if a doctor says that you can. You should only take them for a short time. This is because opioids can be hard to stop taking (they are addictive). The longer you take opioids, the harder it may be to stop taking them (opioid use disorder).

What are the risks?

Opioids can cause problems (side effects). Taking them for more than 3 days raises your chance of problems, such as:

° Trouble pooping (constipation).

° Feeling sick to your stomach (nausea).

° Vomiting.

° Feeling very sleepy.

° Confusion.

° Not being able to stop taking the medicine. ° Breathing problems.

Taking opioids for a long time can make it hard for you to do daily tasks. It can also put you at risk for:

. Car accidents.

° Depression.

. Suicide.

° Heart attack.

° Taking too much of the medicine (overdose), which can sometimes lead to death.

What is a pain treatment plan?

A pain treatment plan is a plan made by you and your doctor. Work with your doctor to make a plan for treating your pain. To help you do this:

° Talk about the goals of your treatment, including:

—46 How much pain you might expect to have. —46 How you will manage the pain. ° Talk about the risks and benefits of taking these medicines for your condition. ° Remember that a good treatment plan uses more than one approach and lowers the risks of side effects. ° Tell your doctor about the amount of medicines you take and about any drug or alcohol use. ° Get your pain medicine prescriptions from only one doctor.

Pain can be managed with other treatments. Work with your doctor to find other ways to help your pain, such as:

. Physical therapy.

° Counseling.

° Eating healthy foods.

° Brain exercises.

° Massage.

° Meditation.

° Other pain medicines. . Doing gentle exercises.

Tapering your use of opioids

If you have been taking opioids for more than a few weeks, you may need to slowly decrease (taper) how much you take until you stop taking them. Doing this can lower your chance of having symptoms, such as:

° Pain and cramping in your belly (abdomen). ° Feeling sick to your stomach.

° Sweating.

° Feeling very sleepy.

° Feeling restless.

° Shaking you cannot control (tremors).

° Cravings for the medicine.

Do not try to stop taking them by yourself. Work with your doctor to stop. Your doctor will help you take less until you are not taking the medicine at all.

Follow these instructions at home:

Safety and storage

° While you are taking opioids: —46 Do not drive. —46 Do not use machines or power tools.

—46 Do not sign important papers (legal documents). —46 Do not drink alcohol. —46 Do not take sleeping pills. —46 Do not take care of children by yourself. —46 Do not do activities where you need to climb or be in high places, like working on a ladder. —46 Do not go into any water, such as a lake, river, ocean, swimming pool, or hot tub. ° Keep your opioids locked up or in a place where children cannot reach them. ° Do not share your pain medicine with anyone.

Getting rid of leftover pills Do not save any leftover pills. Get rid of leftover pills safely by:

° Taking them to a take-back program in your area.

. Bringing them to a pharmacy that has a container for throwing away pills (pill disposal).

° Throwing them in the trash. Check the label or package insert of your medicine to see whether this is safe to do. If it is safe to throw them out:

1. Take the pills out of their container. 2. Mix the pills with pet poop or food scraps. 3. Put this in the trash.

Activity

° Return to your normal activities as told by your doctor. Ask your doctor what activities are safe for you. . Avoid doing things that make your pain worse. ° Do exercises as told by your doctor.

General instructions

. You may need to take these actions to prevent or treat trouble pooping: —46 Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) pale yellow. —46 Take over-the-counter or prescription medicines. —46 Eat foods that are high in fiber. These include beans, whole grains, and fresh fruits and vegetables. —46 Limit foods that are high in fat and sugar. These include fried or sweet foods. ° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Where to find support

If you have been taking opioids for a long time, think about getting help quitting from a local support group or counselor. Ask your doctor about this.

Where to find more information Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC): www.cdc.gov

Get help right away if:

Seek medical care right away if you are taking opioids and you, or people close to you, notice any of the following:

° Your breathing is slower or more shallow than normal.

If you think that you or someone else may have taken too much of an opioid medicine, get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

If you ever feel like you may hurt yourself or others, or have thoughts about taking your own life, get help right away. You can go to your nearest emergency department or call:

. Your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.).

. The hotline of the National Poison Control Center (1-[PHONE-2218] in the U.S.).

. A suicide crisis helpline, such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-[PHONE-2219]. This is open 24 hours a day.

Summary

° Opioid are strong medicines that are used to treat bad or very bad pain.

° A pain treatment plan is a plan made by you and your doctor. Work with your doctor to make a plan for treating your pain. ° Work with your doctor to find other ways to help your pain.

° If you think that you or someone else may have taken too much of an opioid, get help right away.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Opioid Overdose Opioids are drugs that are often used to treat pain. Opioids include illegal drugs, such as heroin, as well as prescription pain medicines, such as codeine, morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone, and fentanyl. An opioid overdose happens when you take too much of an opioid. An overdose may be intentional or accidental and can happen with any type of opioid.

The effects of an overdose can be mild, dangerous, or even deadly. Opioid overdose is a medical emergency. What are the causes?

This condition may be caused by:

. Taking too much of an opioid on purpose.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 802 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2222] Account #: T[PHONE-2223]

Discharge Planning

° Taking too much of an opioid by accident.

° Using two or more substances that contain opioids at the same time.

° Taking an opioid with a substance that affects your heart, breathing, or blood pressure. These include alcohol, tranquilizers, sleeping pills, illegal drugs, and some over-the-counter medicines.

This condition may also happen due to an error made by:

. A health care provider who prescribes a medicine. ° The pharmacist who fills the prescription order.

What increases the risk? This condition is more likely in:

. Children. They may be attracted to colorful pills. Because of a child's small size, even a small amount of a drug can be dangerous. ° Older people. They may be taking many different drugs. Older people may have difficulty reading labels or remembering when they last took their medicine. They may also be more sensitive to the effects of opioids. ° People with chronic medical conditions, especially heart, liver, kidney, or neurological diseases. . People who take an opioid for a long period of time. ° People who use: —46 Illegal drugs. IV heroin is especially dangerous. —46 Other substances, including alcohol, while using an opioid. ° People who have: —46 A history of drug or alcohol abuse. —46 Certain mental health conditions. —46 A history of previous drug overdoses. ° People who take opioids that are not prescribed for them.

What are the signs or symptoms? Symptoms of this condition depend on the type of opioid and the amount that was taken. Common symptoms include:

° Sleepiness or difficulty waking from sleep.

° Decrease in attention.

° Confusion.

° Slurred speech.

. Slowed breathing and a slow pulse (bradycardia). ° Nausea and vomiting.

° Abnormally small pupils.

Signs and symptoms that require emergency treatment include:

° Cold, clammy, and pale skin.

. Blue lips and fingernails.

° Vomiting.

° Gurgling sounds in the throat.

. A pulse that is very slow or difficult to detect.

° Breathing that is very irregular, slow, noisy, or difficult to detect.

. Limp body.

° Inability to respond to speech or be awakened from sleep (stupor). . Seizures.

How is this diagnosed? This condition is diagnosed based on your symptoms and medical history. It is important to tell your health care provider:

¢ About all of the opioids that you took. . When you took the opioids. ° Whether you were drinking alcohol or using marijuana, cocaine, or other drugs.

Your health care provider will do a physical exam. This exam may include:

° Checking and monitoring your heart rate and rhythm, breathing rate, temperature, and blood pressure (vital signs). . Measuring oxygen levels in your blood. . Checking for abnormally small pupils.

You may also have blood tests or urine tests. You may have X-rays if you are having severe breathing problems.

How is this treated?

This condition requires immediate medical treatment and hospitalization. Treatment is given in the hospital intensive care (ICU) setting. Supporting your vital signs and your breathing is the first step in treating an opioid overdose. Treatment may also include:

° Giving salts and minerals (electrolytes) along with fluids through an IV. ° Inserting a breathing tube (endotracheal tube) in your airway to help you breathe if you cannot breathe on your own or you are in danger of not being able to breathe on your own. ° Giving oxygen through a small tube under your nose. ° Passing a tube through your nose and into your stomach (nasogastric tube, or NG tube) to empty your stomach. ° Giving medicines that: —46 Increase your blood pressure. —46 Relieve nausea and vomiting. —46 Relieve abdominal pain and cramping. —46 Reverse the effects of the opioid (naloxone). ° Monitoring your heart and oxygen levels. ° Ongoing counseling and mental health support if you intentionally overdosed or used an illegal drug.

Follow these instructions at home:

Pale yellow Medicines

° Always ask your health care provider about possible side effects and interactions of any new medicine that you start taking.

° Keep a list of all the medicines that you take, including over-the-counter medicines. Bring this list with you to all your medical visits.

General instructions

. Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. ° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

How is this prevented?

° Read the drug inserts that come with your opioid pain medicines.

° Take medicines only as told by your health care provider. Do not take more medicine than you are told. Do not take medicines more frequently than you are told.

. Do not drink alcohol or take sedatives when taking opioids.

° Do not use illegal or recreational drugs, including cocaine, ecstasy, and marijuana.

° Do not take opioid medicines that are not prescribed for you.

° Store all medicines in safety containers that are out of the reach of children.

° Get help if you are struggling with: —46 Alcohol or drug use. —46 Depression or another mental health problem. —46 Thoughts of hurting yourself or another person.

° Keep the phone number of your local poison control center near your phone or in your mobile phone. In the U.S., the hotline of the National Poison Control Center is [PHONE-2228].

° If you were prescribed naloxone, make sure you understand how to take it.

Contact a health care provider if you:

. Need help understanding how to take your pain medicines.

. Feel your medicines are too strong.

. Are concerned that your pain medicines are not working well for your pain. ° Develop new symptoms or side effects when you are taking medicines.

Get help right away if:

° You or someone else is having symptoms of an opioid overdose. Get help even if you are not sure. ° You have serious thoughts about hurting yourself or others. ° You have:

—46 Chest pain.

—46 Difficulty breathing.

—46 A loss of consciousness.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

If you ever feel like you may hurt yourself or others, or have thoughts about taking your own life, get help right away. You can go to your nearest emergency department or call:

. Your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). . A suicide crisis helpline, such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-[PHONE-2231]. This is open 24 hours a day.

Summary

° Opioids are drugs that are often used to treat pain. Opioids include illegal drugs, such as heroin, as well as prescription pain medicines.

° An opioid overdose happens when you take too much of an opioid.

. Overdoses can be intentional or accidental.

° Opioid overdose is very dangerous. It is a life-threatening emergency.

° If you or someone you know is experiencing an opioid overdose, get help right away.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Musculoskeletal Pain

Musculoskeletal pain refers to aches and pains in your bones, joints, muscles, and the tissues that surround them. This pain can occur in any part of the body. It can last for a short time (acute) or a long time (chronic).

A physical exam, lab tests, and imaging studies may be done to find the cause of your musculoskeletal pain.

Follow these instructions at home:

Lifestyle

° Try to control or lower your stress levels. Stress increases muscle tension and can worsen musculoskeletal pain. It is important to recognize when you are anxious or stressed and learn ways to manage it. This may include: —46 Meditation or yoga. —46 Cognitive or behavioral therapy.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 806 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

Discharge Planning

—46 Acupuncture or massage therapy. ° You may continue all activities unless the activities cause more pain. When the pain gets better, slowly resume your normal activities. Gradually increase the intensity and duration of your activities or exercise.

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

. Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. ° When your pain is severe, bed rest may be helpful. Lie or sit in any position that is comfortable, but get out of bed and walk around at least every couple of hours. ° If directed, apply heat to the affected area as often as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source that your health care provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. —46 Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. —46 Remove the heat if your skin turns bright red. This is especially important if you are unable to feel pain, heat, or cold. You may have a greater risk of getting burned. ° If directed, put ice on the painful area. —46 Put ice in a plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

General instructions

° Your health care provider may recommend that you see a physical therapist. This person can help you come up with a safe exercise program. Do any exercises as told by your physical therapist. . Keep all follow-up visits, including any physical therapy visits, as told by your health care providers. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if:

° Your pain gets worse.

. Medicines do not help ease your pain.

° You cannot use the part of your body that hurts, such as your arm, leg, or neck. . You have trouble sleeping.

° You have trouble doing your normal activities.

Get help right away if:

° You have a new injury and your pain is worse or different. ° You feel numb or you have tingling in the painful area.

Summary

° Musculoskeletal pain refers to aches and pains in your bones, joints, muscles, and the tissues that surround them.

° This pain can occur in any part of the body.

° Your health care provider may recommend that you see a physical therapist. This person can help you come up with a safe exercise program. Do any exercises as told by your physical therapist.

° Lower your stress level. Stress can worsen musculoskeletal pain. Ways to lower stress may include meditation, yoga, cognitive or behavioral therapy, acupuncture, and massage therapy.

How to Use Cold Therapy

Cold therapy, or cryotherapy, is a treatment that uses cold temperatures to treat an injury or medical condition. It includes using cold packs or ice packs to reduce pain and swelling. Only use cold therapy if your doctor says it is okay.

What are the risks?

Generally, cold therapy is a safe treatment. However, it is not safe for:

. People who are not able to say they are in pain. These include small children and people who have memory problems. ° People who have certain conditions, such as:

—46 A problem in the vessels that slows blood flow to the fingers and toes (Raynaud's syndrome).

—46 Feeling very cold easily (cold hypersensitivity).

—46 Lack of feeling in the area being iced.

Cold therapy may not be safe for people who have other conditions. Do not use it without talking to your doctor if you have:

. A heart condition.

° High blood pressure.

° Open or healing wounds.

° An infection.

° Pain and swelling in your joints (rheumatoid arthritis). ° Poor blood flow in the body.

° Diabetes.

° Certain skin conditions.

How can | make a cold pack? When using a cold pack at home to reduce pain and swelling, you can use:

. A silica gel cold pack that has been left in the freezer. You can buy this online or in stores.

° A sealable plastic bag that has been filled with crushed ice.

° A washcloth or paper towels soaked in cold (or ice) water.

° A plastic bag of frozen vegetables. Throw them away when you are finished using them as a cold pack.

Supplies needed:

° A cold pack. ° A towel. This can be dry or damp, based on what you like.

How to use cold therapy

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 808 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

Discharge Planning

1. Have your cold pack ready. 2. Place a towel between the cold pack and your skin. You may also wrap the cold pack in a towel. 3. Put the cold pack on the affected area. Keep it on for no more than 20 minutes at a time. 4. Check your skin after 5 minutes to make sure that there is no damage to the area. Check for: ° White spots on your skin. Your skin may look blotchy or mottled. ° Skin that looks blue or pale. ° Skin that feels waxy or hard.

5. Repeat these steps as many times each day as told by your doctor.

Always use a towel to avoid direct contact with your skin. Contact a doctor if:

. You start to have white spots on your skin. This may give your skin a blotchy or mottled look. . Your skin turns blue or pale.

° Your skin becomes waxy or hard.

. Your swelling gets worse.

Summary

° Cold therapy, or cryotherapy, is used to treat an injury or other conditions. It includes using cold packs or ice packs to reduce pain and swelling.

° Cold therapy is not safe for people who are not able to say they are in pain.

° When using cold packs or ice packs, always place a towel between the cold source and your skin.

° Check your skin after 5 minutes of icing it. This is to make sure that there is no skin damage.

° Contact your doctor if you notice changes in your skin or your swelling gets worse.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Follow these instructions at home:

Eating and drinking

° Eat foods that have a lot of fiber, such as: —46 Fresh fruits and vegetables. —46 Whole grains. —46 Beans. ° Eat less of foods that are high in fat, low in fiber, or overly processed, such as: —46 French fries. —46 Hamburgers. —46 Cookies. —46 Candy. —46 Soda. ° Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) clear or pale yellow.

General instructions

Do not hold it in. ° Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor. These include any fiber supplements. ° Do pelvic floor retraining exercises, such as: —46 Doing deep breathing while relaxing your lower belly (abdomen). —46 Relaxing your pelvic floor while pooping. ° Watch your condition for any changes. ° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Contact a doctor if:

. You have pain that gets worse.

° You have a fever.

° You have not pooped for 4 days. . You throw up (vomit).

° You are not hungry.

° You lose weight.

° You are bleeding from the anus.

Elsevier Patient Education © 2020 Elsevier Inc. Walking Boot, Adult

A walking boot is a medical device that holds your foot or ankle in place after an injury or a medical procedure. This helps with healing and prevents further injury. A walking boot is a removable boot-shaped splint. It has a hard, rigid outer frame that limits movement and supports your foot and leg. The inner lining is a layer of padded material. Walking boots usually have adjustable straps to secure them over the foot and leg.

Your health care provider may prescribe a walking boot if you can put weight (bear weight) on your injured foot. How much you can walk while wearing the boot will depend on the type and severity of your injury. Your health care provider will recommend the best boot for you based on your condition.

How do! put on my walking boot?

There are different types of walking boots. Each type of boot has specific instructions about how to wear it properly. Follow instructions from your health care provider about wearing your boot. In general:

° Ask someone to help you put on the boot, if needed.

° Sit to put on your boot. Doing this is more comfortable and it helps to prevent falls.

° Open up the boot fully. Place your foot into the boot so your heel rests against the back.

° Your toes should be supported by the base of the boot. They should not hang over the front edge. ° Adjust the straps so the boot fits securely but is not too tight.

° Do not bend the hard frame of the boot to get a good fit.

What are some tips for walking with a walking boot?

° Do not try to walk without wearing the boot unless your health care provider has approved.

Use other assistive walking devices, including crutches and canes, as told by your health care provider. ° On your uninjured foot, wear a shoe with a heel that is close to the height of the walking boot.

° Be careful when walking on surfaces that are uneven or wet.

How can | reduce swelling while using a walking boot?

° Rest your injured foot or leg as much as possible.

° If directed, apply ice to the injured area: —46 Put ice in a plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

each day or as told by your health care provider. . If swelling gets worse, loosen the boot and rest and elevate your foot and leg.

How should | take care of my skin and foot while using a walking boot?

° Wear a long sock to protect your foot and leg from rubbing inside the boot.

are no sores, rashes, swelling, or wounds. The skin should be a healthy color, not pale or blue.

Try to notice if your walking pattern (gait) in the boot is fairly normal and that you are not walking with a noticeable limp. Follow instructions from your health care provider about taking care of your incision or wound, if this applies.

° Clean and wash the injured area as told by your health care provider.

° Gently dry your foot and leg before putting the boot back on.

Are there any activity restrictions?

Activity restrictions depend on the type and severity of your injury. Follow instructions from your health care provider about limiting activities.

° Bathe and shower as told by your health care provider.

° Do not do any activities that could make your injury worse.

Discharge Planning

° Do not drive if your affected foot is one that you usually use for driving.

When can I! remove my walking boot?

Always follow specific directions from your health care provider for removing the walking boot. Generally, it is okay to remove your walking boot:

. At the end of the day when you are resting or sleeping. . To clean your foot and leg.

How should I keep my walking boot clean?

. Do not put any part of the boot in a washing machine or dryer.

° Do not use chemical cleaning products. These could irritate your skin, especially if you have a wound or an incision. ° Do not soak the liner of the boot.

. Use a washcloth with mild soap and water to clean the frame and the liner of the boot by hand.

° Allow the boot to air-dry completely before you put it back on your foot.

Contact a health care provider if:

° The boot is cracked or damaged.

° The boot does not fit properly.

° Your foot or leg hurts.

° You have a rash, sore, or open sore (ulcer) on your foot or leg. ° The skin on your foot or leg is pale.

. You have a wound or incision on the foot and it is getting worse. ° Your skin becomes painful, red, or irritated.

° Your swelling does not get better or it gets worse.

Get help right away if:

° You cannot feel your foot or leg (have numbness). ° You cannot feel a pulse at the top of your foot, where your foot and ankle meet. . Your skin on the foot or leg is cold, blue, or gray.

Summary

° A walking boot holds your foot or ankle in place after an injury or a medical procedure. . There are different types of walking boots. Follow the specific instructions about how to correctly wear the boot that you have. ° Ask someone to help you put on the boot, if needed. ° It is important to check your skin and foot every day. Call your health care provider if you notice a rash or sore on your foot or

leg.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Ankle Fracture

Tibia tre i Fibula ~. Talus = wn

The ankle joint is made up of the lower (distal) sections of your lower leg bones(tibia and fibula) along with a bone in your foot (talus). An ankle fracture is a break in one, two, or all three of these sections of bone.

Follow these instructions at home: If you have a splint:

° Wear the splint as told by your doctor. Take it off only as told by your doctor. ° Loosen the splint if your toes tingle, become numb, or turn cold and blue. ° Keep the splint clean. ° If the splint is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet. —46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower.

If you have a cast:

° Do not stick anything inside the cast to scratch your skin. Doing that increases your risk of infection. ° Check the skin around the cast every day. Tell your doctor about any concerns. . You may put lotion on dry skin around the edges of the cast. Do not put lotion on the skin underneath the cast. ° Keep the cast clean. ° If the cast is not waterproof: —46 Do not let it get wet. —46 Cover it with a watertight covering when you take a bath or a shower.

Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

° If directed, put ice on the injured area: —46 If you have a removable splint, remove it as told by your doctor. —46 Put ice in a plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. ° Move your toes often. This prevents stiffness and lessens swelling. . Raise (elevate) the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down.

General instructions

Discharge Planning

° Do not use the injured limb to support your body weight until your doctor says that you can. Use crutches as told by your doctor.

° Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor.

° Ask your doctor when it is safe to drive if you have a cast or splint.

° Do exercises as told by your doctor.

° Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. These can delay bone healing. If you need help quitting, ask your doctor. ° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Contact a doctor if:

. Your pain or swelling gets worse. ° Your pain or swelling does not get better when you rest or take medicine.

Get help right away if:

° Your cast gets damaged.

. You continue to have very bad pain.

. You have new pain or swelling.

. Your skin or toes below the injured ankle: —46 Turn blue or gray. —46 Feel cold or numb. —46 Lose sensitivity to touch.

Summary

° An ankle fracture is a break in one, two, or all three of the bones in your lower leg and lower foot.

° If you have a splint, wear it as told by your health care provider. Keep it clean and dry.

° If you have a cast, do not stick anything inside the cast to scratch your skin. This can cause infection.

. Use ice, take medicines, raise your foot, and avoid tobacco and nicotine products. These steps will lessen pain and swelling and speed up healing.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

° If directed, put ice on the affected area. —46 If you have a removable boot, remove it as told by your health care provider. —46 Put ice in a plastic bag. —46 Place a towel between your skin and the bag or between your cast and the bag. —46 Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day.

. Move your toes often to avoid stiffness and to lessen swelling.

. Raise (elevate) the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down. To do this, try putting a few pillows under your leg and ankle.

Driving . Do not drive or use heavy machinery while taking prescription pain medicine. * Ask your health care provider when it is safe to drive if you have a boot or cast on your foot.

Activity

° Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you.

° Do exercises as told by your health care provider or physical therapist.

° Do not use your injured limb to support (bear) your body weight until your health care provider says that you can. Follow weight-bearing restrictions as told. Use crutches or other devices to help you move around (assistive devices) as directed.

General instructions ° Do not put pressure on any part of the cast until it is fully hardened. This may take several hours.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 817 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2265] Account #: T[PHONE-2266]

Discharge Planning

° Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. These can delay bone healing. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

. Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

. If you are taking prescription pain medicine, take actions to prevent or treat constipation. Your health care provider may recommend that you: —46 Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. —46 Eat foods that are high in fiber, such as fresh fruits and vegetables, whole grains, and beans. —46 Limit foods that are high in fat and processed sugars, such as fried or sweet foods. —46 Take an over-the-counter or prescription medicine for constipation.

° Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if:

° You have a fever.

° Your pain medicine is not helping.

° You have redness around your incision.

. You have more swelling or pain around your incision.

° You have blood or more fluid coming from your incision or leaking through your cast. ° Your incision feels warm to the touch.

. You have pus or a bad smell coming from your incision or dressings.

Get help right away if:

. The edges of your incision come apart after the stitches or staples have been taken out. ° You have chest pain.

* You have difficulty breathing.

. You have numbness or tingling in your foot or leg.

° Your foot becomes cold, pale, or blue.

Summary

° After the procedure, it is common to have some pain and swelling.

° If your boot or cast is not waterproof, do not let it get wet.

° Contact your health care provider if you have severe pain or swelling, or if you have more fluids coming from your incision or leaking through your cast.

° Get help right away if you have numbness or tingling in your foot or leg, or if your foot becomes cold, pale, or blue.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Displaced Medial or Posterior Malleolar Ankle Fracture Treated With ORIF

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 818 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2269] Account #: T[PHONE-2270]

Discharge Planning

~ Fracture

A malleolar fracture is a specific break in the large bone in the lower leg (tibia). The medial malleolus is the bottom end of the tibia. You feel it as the bump on the inner side of your ankle. The posterior malleolus is the lower-rear part of the tibia that is closest to your heel.

Open reduction with internal fixation (ORIF) is a surgical procedure that may be used to treat a malleolar fracture. You may need this surgery if your fracture is displaced, which means that the bones are not lined up correctly and the ankle joint is not stable. During ORIF, a surgeon will move the bones back into the correct positions. Then, the surgeon will put in a combination of screws, metal plates, or different types of wiring to hold the bones in place.

Tell a health care provider about:

. Any allergies you have.

° All medicines you are taking, including vitamins, herbs, eye drops, creams, and over-the-counter medicines. . Any problems you or family members have had with anesthetic medicines.

. Any blood disorders you have.

° Any surgeries you have had.

. Any medical conditions you have.

° Whether you are pregnant or may be pregnant.

What are the risks?

Generally, this is a safe procedure. However, problems may occur, including:

° Excessive bleeding.

° Infection.

. Allergic reactions to medicines.

. Damage to nerves or blood vessels.

° Failure of the fracture to heal.

. Long-term pain and stiffness (arthritis). ° Stiffness in the ankle after the repair.

What happens before the procedure?

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 819 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2271] Account #: T[PHONE-2272]

Discharge Planning

Staying hydrated Follow instructions from your health care provider about hydration, which may include:

° Up to 2 hours before the procedure — you may continue to drink clear liquids, such as water, clear fruit juice, black coffee, and plain tea.

Eating and drinking restrictions Follow instructions from your health care provider about eating and drinking, which may include:

° 8 hours before the procedure — stop eating heavy meals or foods such as meat, fried foods, or fatty foods. ° 6 hours before the procedure — stop eating light meals or foods, such as toast or cereal.

° 6 hours before the procedure — stop drinking milk or drinks that contain milk.

° 2 hours before the procedure — stop drinking clear liquids.

Medicines

° Ask your health care provider about:

—46 Changing or stopping your regular medicines. This is especially important if you are taking diabetes medicines or blood thinners.

—46 Taking medicines such as aspirin and ibuprofen. These medicines can thin your blood. Do not take these medicines unless your health care provider tells you to take them. —46 Taking over-the-counter medicines, vitamins, herbs, and supplements. . You may be given antibiotic medicine to help prevent infection.

General instructions

. Plan to have someone take you home from the hospital or clinic.

° Plan to have a responsible adult care for you for at least 24 hours after you leave the hospital or clinic. This is important. ° Ask your health care provider how your surgical site will be marked or identified.

. You may be asked to shower with a germ-killing soap.

What happens during the procedure?

. To lower your risk of infection:

—46 Your health care team will wash or sanitize their hands.

—46 Hair may be removed from the surgical area.

—46 Your skin will be washed with soap. ° An IV will be inserted into one of your veins. You may be given antibiotic medicine and medicine for pain through the IV. . You will be given one or more of the following:

—46 A medicine to numb the area (local anesthetic).

—46 A medicine to make you fall asleep (general anesthetic).

—46 A medicine that is injected into your spine to numb the area below and slightly above the injection site (spinal anesthetic).

—46 A medicine that is injected into an area of your body to numb everything below the injection site (regional anesthetic). ° The surgeon will make an incision through your skin over the area of the fracture.

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 820 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST




ULH University of Louisville Hospital

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2273] Account #: T[PHONE-2274]

Discharge Planning

° The broken bones will be put back into their normal positions. The surgeon will use a combination of screws, metal plates, or different types of wiring to hold the bones in place.

° After the bones are back in place, the surgeon will close the incision using stitches (sutures) or staples.

° A bandage (dressing) and a cast or supportive boot will be placed over your ankle.

The procedure may vary among health care providers and hospitals. What happens after the procedure?

° Your blood pressure, heart rate, breathing rate, and blood oxygen level will be monitored until the medicines you were given have worn off.

* — You will be given medicine for pain as needed.

° You may be given instructions about how much body weight you can or cannot safely support (bear) on your injured foot

(weight-bearing restrictions). You may be given crutches, a cane, or a walker to help you move around so that you do not bear any weight on your foot.

° While in the hospital, you will be helped out of bed so you can begin moving around. This is important to improve blood flow and breathing.

° Do not drive for 24 hours if you were given a medicine to help you relax (sedative) during your procedure.

Summary

° A malleolar fracture is a specific break in the large bone in the lower leg (tibia).

° The fracture may occur in the inner (medial) side of your ankle or in the lower-rear (posterior) part of your ankle near your heel.

. Your surgeon will repair this break by making an incision, moving the bones back into the correct position, and using screws, metal plates, or wires to hold the bones in place.

° The incision will be closed with stitches (sutures) or staples, and a cast or supportive boot will be placed over your ankle.

° Plan to have someone take you home after the procedure. Also, plan to have a responsible adult care for you for at least 24 hours after you leave the hospital or clinic.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Emergency Awareness and Preventative Care

STROKE is an EMERGENCY Every Minute Counts Act FAST and Check for these signs:

FACE Does the face look uneven?

ARM Does one arm drift down?

Discharge Planning

SPEECH Does their speech sound strange?

TIME Call 9-1-1 at any sign of stroke

Stroke Risk Factors

Atrial Fibrillation (irregular heartbeat) Diabetes Family history of stroke Heart Disease Heavy alcohol use High Blood Pressure High Cholesterol Physical inactivity and obesity Smoking

Cigarette Smoking

The facts are clear, cigarette smoking will shorten your life. Smoking can cause many illnesses along the way. As a healthcare provider, we recommend that you stop smoking. Assistance with quitting is available by contacting 1-800-QUIT-NOW. This is a free resource providing counseling, support, and referral. Or you may contact your personal physician.

National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 1-[PHONE-2279] The National Suicide Prevention Lifeline is a national network of local crisis centers that provides free and confidential emotional support to people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

Don't Wait! Stop a Heart Attack Before it Starts What is a heart attack?

A heart attack is damage or death to a part of the heart from severely decreased or lack of blood flow to the heart. Over time, arteries can become narrow from the buildup of fat and cholesterol, which is called plaque. The plaque can rupture causing a blood clot to form. When the blood clot forms, the artery can become severely narrowed or completely blocked, causing a heart attack.

¢ Heart attack is the leading cause of death in the United States. * 85% of muscle damage occurs within the first 2 hours. * Delay in the recognition of heart attack symptoms increases the chances of death.

Know the early symptoms of a heart attack:

Nausea Feeling of “fullness” in chest Jaw Pain Pain that travels down one or both arms

Fatigue/being tired Anxiety Back Pain Chest pressure, squeezing, or discomfort

Shortness of breath Sweating, or a cold sweat Feeling of impending doom

There are unusual signs of a heart attack, too! Women, the elderly, and diabetics may present with atypical symptoms:

* Fainting/dizziness ¢« Weakness * Confusion

Risk Factors for a Heart Attack Some heart disease risk factors, such as age and family history, cannot be changed. Others, like smoking and lack of exercise, can be changed.

Smoking High Cholesterol High Blood Pressure Family History Obesity Age

Gender (Males are at higher risk) Lack of Exercise Diabetes

Diet Stress Excessive Alcohol Intake

If you or someone you know is experiencing the signs and symptoms of a heart attack, DON?T DELAY. Call 9-1-1 immediately and seek help. If someone collapses, perform CPR!

LEGEND: *=Corrected, @=Abnormal, C=Critical, L=Low, H=High, f=Footnote, #=Interpretive Data, R=Ref Lab Report Request ID: 143290767 Page 822 of 1,092 12/3/2021 12:58 EST

Patient: DOWNS, JUANITA NECOLE DOB/Age/Sex: 5/14/1998 23 years Female MRN: [PHONE-2280] Account #: T[PHONE-2281]

Discharge Planning

Do not attempt to drive if you are having symptoms of heart attack.

Hands-Only CPR Why Hands-Only CPR?

* Hands-Only CPR has been shown to be as effective as conventional CPR for cardiac arrests that occur outside of a hospital. * Survival depends on immediately receiving CPR from someone nearby.

How do you perform Hands-Only CPR? There are two easy steps:

1. Call 9-1-1 if you see a teen or adult collapse 2. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest at a beat of 100 beats per minute.

Save a life!

4 WAYS TO GET AHEAD OF SEPSIS SEPSIS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Time matters!

Infections put you and your family at risk for a life-threatening condition called sepsis.

Sepsis is the body's extreme response to an infection. It is life-threatening, and without timely treatment, sepsis can rapidly lead to tissue damage, organ failure, and death. Sepsis happens when an infection you already have-in your skin, lungs, urinary tract or somewhere else-triggers a chain reaction throughout your body.

PREVENT * Take good care of chronic conditions. INFECTIONS ¢ Talk to your doctor about getting the recommended vaccines. PRACTICE ¢ Wash your hands frequently.

GOOD * Keep cuts or open sores clean and covered until they are HYGIENE healed.

¢ Confusion or disorientation

* Shortness of breath

* High heart rate

* Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold « Extreme pain or discomfort

« Clammy or sweaty skin

To learn more about sepsis and how to prevent infections, visit www.cdc.gov/sepsis.

INFORMED CONSENT PATIENT EDUCATIONAL INFORMATION

Informed consent: Your doctor may determine that a blood transfusion is needed as part of your medical care. It is important that you or your legal representative understand the risks and benefits of receiving a blood component transfusion. Your doctor will explain (or has explained) to you or your legal representative the risks and benefits of a blood component transfusion and any alternative treatment(s) to a blood transfusion. The following information on blood and blood components is being given to you or your legal representative to assist you or your legal representative in making an informed decision about receiving a blood transfusion. Before you or your legal representative sign the informed consent to receive a blood transfusion, your doctor or medical professional will be available to answer any questions you may have about blood and biood transfusions. Please ask these individuals to explain anything that you did not understand about the risks and benefits of the blood transfusion you may require.

Benefits: Tests performed during your hospitalization may show that you need blood. Blood is made of many elements. Which elements you should get and the amount to be given are decisions made only by your doctor. :

¢ Red Blood Cells ~ carry oxygen to your tissues and organs Granulocytes - white cells needed to fight infection

¢ Platelets — help clot your blood

» Cryoprecipitate - several proteins needed to clot your blood

e Plasma - liquid portion assists in clotting your blood ¢ Albumin — protein needed in your plasma e Factor Concentrates - assist in clotting your blood e Rh(D) Immune Globulin — used to protect against Hemolytic Disesase of the Newbom

Testing the blood you receive: Volunteer donors, who undergo strict screening, donate blood. Many tests (in addition to blood type) are performed on this

blood and tested by the blood supplier:

¢ Hepatitis B and C (viruses that injure the liver)

¢ HIV (viruses that cause AIDS)

¢ HTLV (human T-cell lymphotrophic viruses which may cause blood and nerve disease) ¢ Syphilis (a sexually transmitted disease)

Any blood donation found to be positive for any of these tests is destroyed. Finally, if you are going to receive red blood cells, the hospital blood blank will test

a sample of your blood and that of the acceptable donated blood unit to make sure that the blood is compatible (crossmatching your blood).

Risks: Blood should be thought of as a drug having certain risks or side effects. Some patients may have a mild reaction to a blood transfusion, suct fever, chills, hives, or body aches. More severe reactions such as breathing difficulties and shock occur very rarely. Human error, though uncomn may also occur during the preparation of your blood component for transfusion. The risk of getting an infectious disease, such as hepatitis or HIV from a bl-w

transfusion is very rare.

¢ Hepatitis B virus ¢ Hepatitis C virus ¢ Other organisms (malaria, Chagas, Babesiosis)

Infectious Agent Risk (per unit)

Infectious Agent Risk (per unit)

1:205,000 HIV 1:2,135,000 1:1,935,000 1:2,993,000 1:1,000,000

You should compare these risks with voluntary or involuntary activities people have:

Alternative Treatments:

Infectious Agent Risk (per unit)

¢ Smoking 1:200

¢ Automobile Driving 1:10,000 e¢ Tornado 1:455,000 e Influenza 1:5,000

¢ Autologous blood transfusion refers to procedures in which you may serve as your own blood donor. In some cases, this may be done prior to your hospitalization. In other cases, blood lost during surgery can be washed and given back to you. (called Intraoperative Cell Salvage)

* Autologous units are reserved for your use and kept until the expiration date of not used by you. They cannot ever be given to another person if not used by you. If this blood is insufficient for your transfusion needs, blood components from other donors will be used.

« In other situations, you may be able to select donors of your blood (family or friends) and receive a directed donation. Studies have shown, however, tha these donations are no safer than that from the community blood supply. .

¢ Directed units that you do not use will be released on the day of your hospital discharge for use by other patients unless your physician notifies the b bank to hold those units for your use in the future or until the expiration date.

* Bloodless medicine/surgery techniques (called Blood Conservation) may be appropriate for selected patients in certain circumstances.

EXPLANATION OF AND CONSENT TO ANESTHESIA

| understand that an attending anesthesiologist who is a member of the faculty of the Department of Anesthesiology and Perioperative Medicine at the University of Louisville School of Medicine will supervise my anesthesia at all times. The attending anesthesiologist may be assisted by anesthesiology residents, certified nurse anesthetists and/or students nurse anesthetists who have been authorized by the Department of Anesthesiology and the University of Louisville Hospital to provide anesthesia care under the supervision of an attending anesthesiologist. | understand that the attending anesthesiologist and other anesthesia providers involved in my care will be identified at the time of surgery and their names will appear on the Anesthesia Pre-Operative Evaluation and Anesthesia Record.

The primary anesthetic planned for my procedure is (CHOOSE ONE ONLY)

XGeneral Anesthesia - being unconcious during the procedure

Q Regional Anesthesia — injections near major nerves that cause temporary numbness - with or without sedation. Sometimes regional anesthesia includes a small tube (catheter) that may be left in place for managing pain after the procedure.

STM

YM Combi ed - od ral anesthesia for the procedure, combined with regional anesthesia for managing pain.

Q Monitored Anesthesia - pain medicines and sedation, sometimes combined with local anesthesia given by the surgeon.

| understand that any anesthesia can result in a need for general anesthesia.

The risks of general anesthesia include nausea, vomiting, headache, sore throat or hoarseness, airway problems, recall of events during the procedure, reactions to medicines, injury to teeth, eyes or nerves, unexpected serious injury, or death.

The risks of regional anesthesia include headache, infection, reaction to medicines, seizures, nerve injury, bleeding or bruising from needle injury, injury to underlying organs, inadequate block or wearing off of block during surgery resulting in the need for general anesthesia.

The anesthesiologist may use the following special monitors during the procedure (CHECK ANY THAT APPLY):

Q) Arterial catheter - a tube placed in an artery to measure blood pressure and collect samples of blood.

O Central venous or pulmonary artery catheter - a tube placed in a vein near the heart to measure heart function.

! understand that my risk of complications will depend on the type of anesthesia and my underlying health status. | also understand that if | am pregnant, | may wish to delay elective surgery.

My planned anesthesia has been explained to me, including expected benefits, potential complications and risks, and the risks and benefits of potential alternative treatments, and my questions answered. | retain the right to withdraw this consent.

Explanation of and Consent to Anesthesia

For patients unable to give consent: Procedure for patients unable to give consent are permitted with specific authorizations in the following situations:

e Amedical or surgical emergency, with imminent threat to life, limb or function: Specify nature of emergency and risk of delay in treatment

Physician’s Signature Date

An authorized designee is required to grant consent for those patients who MAY NOT or CANNOT sign their own consent form.

An authorized designee is designated in order of authority:

a. Court appointed guardian with medical decision authority, a person designated by durable power of attorney for health care or other health care surrogate designation . Spouse . Adult child, or, if more than one, a majority of available adult children. . Parents

. Nearest living relative, or, if more than one, a majority of the nearest living relatives of the same relation. Signature of Surrogate Decision-Maker Witness to Signature of Surrogate Decision-Maker —_ Relationship to Patient

e Aminor patient (under 18) without legal decision-making capacity:

Signature of Parent/Legal Guardian Witness to Signature of Parent/Legal Guardian

Verbal Consent obtained:

For non-emergency cases in which an authorized designee is required to grant consent, but no authorized designee is present:

e A verbal consent may be obtained over the telephone, verified by two witnesses listening (The witnesses should include the physician and a member of the patient's family or friend who can attest to the identity of the person providing consent).

e The authorized designee should be instructed to send written consent either by telegram, fax, or overnight mail service to the Health Information Management Department immediately.

Family/Friend Witness

University of Louisville Hospital J James Graham Brown Cancer Center

The term “UofL Health” means UofL Health at any of its hospitals, offices, andlor her locations as well as all of University of Louisville Physicians, nurses, and other staff, employees or affiliates.

| | give my consent to UofL Health providing care and treatment, including consultation, during the course of my admission to UofL Health. By signing this consent form, | consent to each of the following:

TREATMENT

| have a condition requiring examination, diagnosis and treatment. | consent to and authorize such care including but not limited to: X-ray, laboratory, routine diagnostic tests and therapeutic procedures ("Services") performed by my admitting and treating physicians.

| acknowledge that not all health care providers including physicians, nurse anesthetists, nurse practitioners, therapists, residents and students, are employees or agents of UofL Health. Such individuals are independent contractors who bill separately for their Services. | also understand that UofL Health is not responsible for nor does it assume any liability for the acts or omissions of any independent contractor(s). | acknowledge there may be supervised participation of health care students (medical students, nursing students, interns, residents and non-physician clinical students).

| understand that my treatment may involve risks and that there are no guarantees concerning the outcome or results of this treatment and/or examination and | have the right to make decisions concerning my health care, including the right to refuse medical and surgical procedures/treatment. |

TESTING FOR INFECTIOUS DISEASES |

| understand that as part of my medical treatment, procedures or testing, | may be tested for Human Immunodeficiency Virus ("HIV"), Hepatitis, or any other blood borne infectious disease as well COVID-19 or any airborne pathogen if a physician or advanced practice registered nurse orders the test for diagnostic purposes. | also understand that, in the case of exposure of a health care provider or first responder (such as an emergency worker, fire fighter or police officer) to my bodily fluids, although | will be informed of the right to consent to testing for HIV or other infectious diseases transmitted by bodily fluids, in most cases, tests may be performed on previously gathered bodily fluid samples or a court order may be obtained to compel such testing. Information concerning the fact that a test was ordered and the results of such test will remain confidential and be disclosed by UofL Health only as permitted by law.

FINANCIAL RESPONSIBILITY

| agree that | obligate myself and/or the Patient to pay the account(s) of UofL Health in accordance with the established rates and payment policy of UofL Health. | also understand that patient/guarantor payments can be applied to any account where payment is due to include any credit balance resulting from benefit payments or other sources.

| understand that if my Insurer denies all or any part of UofL Health’s charges for any reason, or if | have no insurance, | will be personally and fully responsible for payment of UofL Health’s charges. | am aware that | must notify the Business Office and submit the completed application form if | believe | may qualify for financial assistance. I/My Designated Health Care Representative authorizes billing by and direct payment to UofL Health or to the physicians providing Services at UofL Health. | understand they will bill my insurance directly for their Services, at a rate not to exceed the UofL Health’s or physician’s regular charges. Insurance includes but is not limited to, my medical health insurance policy/policies, including my employee health benefit plan, accident, worker's compensation benefits, motor vehicle insurance, casualty insurance, and uninsured or underinsured insurance
